answer: The directive sets reduction targets but not absolute bans. Countries like Malta, Cyprus, and Greece received longer transitional periods. Failure to meet the 2016 BMW target of 35% of 1995 levels led the Commission to open infringement cases — Greece was referred to the CJEU in 2023 for still landfilling 80% of its waste.
title: Council Directive 1999/31/EC on the landfill of waste

question: What is the 2035 limit on landfilling and how does it connect to these waste rules?
answer: The 2018 amendment (Directive (EU) 2018/850) set a strict limit: by 2035, no Member State may landfill more than 10% of its municipal waste. This is an absolute cap, unlike the original 1999 directive's relative targets. Countries that still landfill heavily — like Romania (69% in 2020) and Croatia (54%) — face massive investment needs to build treatment infrastructure and will likely miss the deadline without derogations.
title: Council Directive 1999/31/EC on the landfill of waste

question: Why do UK employee transfer protections still matter after Brexit?
answer: The UK's TUPE regulations remain based on the 2001 directive, and the UK retained them after Brexit under the European Union (Withdrawal) Act 2018. However, the UK government has consulted on reducing TUPE obligations for businesses, and EU case law — including ECJ rulings — no longer binds UK courts, creating potential divergence.
title: Council Directive 2001/23/EC on the approximation of the laws of the Member States relating to the safeguarding of employees' rights in the event of transfers of undertakings, businesses or parts of undertakings or businesses

question: Why aren't all workers protected from being fired when their workplace changes hands?
answer: The directive allows dismissals for "economic, technical or organisational reasons" (ETO reasons) that require changes in the workforce. This exception, heavily lobbied for by employer groups like BUSINESSEUROPE, permits redundancy if the new employer can prove the dismissal is unrelated to the transfer itself. The Commission's 2013 report found that this exception was the most litigated provision, with employee representatives arguing it was too easily abused.
title: Council Directive 2001/23/EC on the approximation of the laws of the Member States relating to the safeguarding of employees' rights in the event of transfers of undertakings, businesses or parts of undertakings or businesses

question: Why don't EU rules on employee rights during business transfers also cover what happens when a company goes bankrupt?
answer: The 2001 directive explicitly allows Member States to decide whether employee rights transfer when a business is transferred from an insolvent entity. This was a political compromise: Germany and France wanted to protect struggling companies from inheriting liabilities, while the UK and Sweden pushed for full protection. The European Parliament's 1997 resolution had called for uniform insolvency rules, but the Council dropped the proposal under pressure from the German Bundesrat.
title: Council Directive 2001/23/EC on the approximation of the laws of the Member States relating to the safeguarding of employees' rights in the event of transfers of undertakings, businesses or parts of undertakings or businesses

question: Why wasn't the EU's emergency mass migration plan used during the 2015 crisis?
answer: The TPD was designed for sudden, short-term mass influxes, not for migration that builds up over months. In 2015, the European Commission under Jean-Claude Juncker considered activating it in September for Syrian refugees, but the proposal never reached a Council vote. Several Member States — particularly Hungary, Poland, and the Czech Republic — refused to accept mandatory quotas, and the political climate was deeply hostile to any EU-imposed relocation. The UK also opposed activation, warning it would "encourage further flows." The Council instead relied on ad-hoc resettlement schemes and bilateral agreements, which proved inadequate.
title: Council Directive 2001/55/EC on minimum standards for giving temporary protection in the event of a mass influx of displaced persons and on measures promoting a balance of efforts between Member States in receiving such persons and bearing the consequences thereof

question: Which countries blocked the EU's temporary protection rules when they were first created in 2001?
answer: The UK, Ireland, and Denmark opted out of the directive entirely. The UK's Home Office at the time expressed concerns that binding burden-sharing would erode national control over borders. Denmark secured an opt-out under its Protocol on Justice and Home Affairs. Among Member States that did participate, negotiations on burden-sharing were the most contentious — the final text made all solidarity measures voluntary, not mandatory.
title: Council Directive 2001/55/EC on minimum standards for giving temporary protection in the event of a mass influx of displaced persons and on measures promoting a balance of efforts between Member States in receiving such persons and bearing the consequences thereof

question: Why did the EU's emergency protection system work for Ukrainian refugees but not for Syrian refugees?
answer: The primary reasons were geographic proximity and political will. Ukraine borders four EU Member States — Poland, Slovakia, Hungary, and Romania — making it possible for large numbers to reach the EU by land. Syrian refugees arriving via the Mediterranean and Aegean faced a far more difficult journey and higher risks of death at sea. Politically, EU leaders saw the Ukraine crisis as an existential threat to European security, and the refugee flows were overwhelmingly women and children, which reduced public resistance. In contrast, the 2015 crisis was framed by many governments as an "invasion" or "security threat." The contrast prompted accusations of double standards from human rights organisations and some Global South governments.
title: Council Directive 2001/55/EC on minimum standards for giving temporary protection in the event of a mass influx of displaced persons and on measures promoting a balance of efforts between Member States in receiving such persons and bearing the consequences thereof

question: What changed between the EU's 2001 temporary protection rules and the 2024 version?
answer: The 2024 regulation replaces the directive and is directly applicable in all Member States, removing the need for national implementing laws. It introduces mandatory solidarity contributions — financial support or relocation — triggered by a qualified majority Council decision, replacing the voluntary system of 2001. Activation is also expedited: the Commission can now propose activation immediately upon receiving evidence of a mass influx, and the Council must vote within two weeks. The regulation also adds new protections including more detailed rules on residence permits, family reunification, and access to employment for beneficiaries.
title: Council Directive 2001/55/EC on minimum standards for giving temporary protection in the event of a mass influx of displaced persons and on measures promoting a balance of efforts between Member States in receiving such persons and bearing the consequences thereof

question: Why did some EU countries strongly oppose the rules on letting immigrants bring their families to join them?
answer: Germany, Austria, and the Netherlands argued that the directive infringed on national sovereignty over immigration and that family reunification could become a "magnet" for irregular migration, especially from Muslim-majority countries. They insisted on integration conditions, such as language tests, as a tool to manage cultural integration and prevent forced marriages. Germany's then-Interior Minister Otto Schily warned in 2002 that the directive would "create legal titles for family members that cannot be revoked," a position that reflected domestic political anxiety after the 9/11 attacks.
title: Council Directive 2003/86/EC on the right to family reunification

question: Why didn't the European Parliament manage to make the EU's family reunification rules more protective of families?
answer: The Parliament voted overwhelmingly for stronger protections in 2000 but was overridden by the Council, which acted under the consultation procedure — meaning MEPs gave non-binding opinion only. The 2006 Court challenge (Case C-540/03) failed to overturn the integration clause because the Court ruled that the directive's objective was legitimate and the clause did not violate the right to family life per se, provided it was applied proportionally. This precedent limited the scope for future legislative revision.
title: Council Directive 2003/86/EC on the right to family reunification

question: Why do the EU's family reunification rules still matter after the 2015 migration crisis?
answer: The directive remains the only EU-wide legal instrument for non-EU residents to bring family members, covering long-term residents (often from former colonies or labour migration schemes). After 2015, political attention shifted to asylum and humanitarian protection, leading to a separate "Family Reunification for Beneficiaries of International Protection" clause in the 2011 Qualification Directive. However, the 2003 directive still governs the vast majority of family reunification cases for third-country nationals — estimated by the Commission in 2019 to cover over one million applications per year across the EU.
title: Council Directive 2003/86/EC on the right to family reunification

question: Does the EU require countries to bury their nuclear waste underground?
answer: No — the directive does not mandate a specific disposal method. It requires each country to adopt a national policy and a programme that “demonstrates that the planned final solution is technically feasible and achievable within a reasonable period.” That can be deep geological disposal, but also long‑term storage in monitored surface facilities, as long as the country explains why that is preferable and how it will eventually move to disposal.
title: Council Directive 2011/70/Euratom establishing a Community framework for the responsible and safe management of spent fuel and radioactive waste

question: Why did it take more than 50 years after the Euratom Treaty for binding waste rules?
answer: The Euratom Treaty (1957) gave the Community competence over health and safety, but member states fiercely guarded their sovereignty over nuclear energy. Non‑binding recommendations in 2004 failed to produce results, and only after the 2009 Council request — combined with growing waste volumes and the 2009 nuclear safety directive creating a precedent — did member states accept legal obligations. The unanimity requirement in the Council meant that France and the UK could veto any text that imposed specific deadlines or technologies.
title: Council Directive 2011/70/Euratom establishing a Community framework for the responsible and safe management of spent fuel and radioactive waste

question: What happens if a country has no disposal facility and no credible plan?
answer: The Commission can open infringement proceedings and ultimately refer the case to the Court of Justice. The directive requires each national programme to include a clear schedule for the construction of disposal facilities. If a country cannot meet its own schedule, it must revise the programme and justify the delay. So far, the Commission has used the infringement procedure as a compliance tool: Italy only finalised its programme after the Commission sent a reasoned opinion.
title: Council Directive 2011/70/Euratom establishing a Community framework for the responsible and safe management of spent fuel and radioactive waste

question: What safety rules apply to EU nuclear plants after Fukushima?
answer: The 2014 Nuclear Safety Directive made it legally binding for every EU country with nuclear reactors to conduct a “stress test” every six years, covering extreme natural events, loss of power, and loss of cooling. Plants must also be subject to a periodic peer review by experts from other EU countries, and the national regulator must be independent from the industry it oversees. New reactors must meet a safety target so stringent that a large radioactive release is considered “practically eliminated” – typically meaning a probability below one in a million per year.
title: Council Directive 2014/87/Euratom of 8 July 2014 amending Directive 2009/71/Euratom establishing a Community framework for the nuclear safety of nuclear installations

question: Did the EU force any nuclear plants to close after Fukushima?
answer: No. The stress tests found no plant that needed immediate shutdown, but they identified more than 80 “findings” requiring safety upgrades at individual reactors. Several older reactors – including two in Belgium and two in Sweden – were voluntarily closed by their operators in the years after the disaster, partly due to the cost of meeting the new requirements. Germany, which decided independently to phase out nuclear by 2022, did so through national law, not EU pressure.
title: Council Directive 2014/87/Euratom of 8 July 2014 amending Directive 2009/71/Euratom establishing a Community framework for the nuclear safety of nuclear installations

question: What was the cost of complying with the post-Fukushima safety rules?
answer: The European Commission’s 2012 impact assessment estimated total compliance costs across the EU fleet at €3 billion to €7 billion over ten years. France alone has allocated about €10 billion for post-Fukushima upgrades. The cost has been passed through to electricity prices in most countries. For a single reactor, upgrades have typically cost €100–€300 million.
title: Council Directive 2014/87/Euratom of 8 July 2014 amending Directive 2009/71/Euratom establishing a Community framework for the nuclear safety of nuclear installations

question: Can the European Commission fine a country for unsafe nuclear plants?
answer: Yes. Under the Euratom Treaty, the Commission can take member states to the European Court of Justice for failing to transpose or implement the directive. In 2023, the Commission opened infringement proceedings against Belgium for not fully transposing the independence requirements for its nuclear regulator. However, the directive does not give the Commission the power to order the closure of a nuclear plant – that remains a national sovereignty issue.
title: Council Directive 2014/87/Euratom of 8 July 2014 amending Directive 2009/71/Euratom establishing a Community framework for the nuclear safety of nuclear installations

question: Why can farmers only spread 170 kg of nitrogen per hectare per year from manure?
answer: The 170 kg N/ha limit was a political compromise reached during the 1991 Council negotiations. Agronomically, it was based on the principle that crops typically cannot absorb more than 170–200 kg of nitrogen per hectare per year — any surplus leaches into groundwater. The limit was also designed to match the highest manure application rates sustainable on arable land without requiring farmers to buy additional land. Critically, the limit applies *only* to manure nitrogen — synthetic fertilisers are not capped, a loophole that farmers in intensive regions continue to exploit. According to the Commission's 2013 implementation report, the limit is "the most contested provision" of the directive, with the Netherlands having repeatedly requested — and secured — a derogation allowing up to 250 kg N/ha given its high-yield pasture systems.
title: Council Directive 91/676/EEC concerning the protection of waters against pollution caused by nitrates from agricultural sources

question: Why did it take almost ten years for EU rules on farm pollution to start working?
answer: The directive entered into force in 1991 but gave Member States until December 1993 for transposition and until December 1998 for action programmes — a seven-year implementation period. This delay was intentional: the directive's architects, particularly DG Environment, recognised that changing agricultural practices required new investments in manure storage, spreading machinery, and crop rotations. However, the main reason for the slow effect was weak enforcement. Several Member States — including Belgium, the Netherlands, and the UK — initially designated only a fraction of their potentially vulnerable zones. It took a series of ECJ judgements — particularly *Commission v. Netherlands* (2003) and *Commission v. Belgium* (2004) — to force Member States to properly designate vulnerable zones. According to the European Court of Auditors' 2021 report, "it was only after 2010 that most Member States had action programmes in place covering the majority of their agricultural area."
title: Council Directive 91/676/EEC concerning the protection of waters against pollution caused by nitrates from agricultural sources

question: Why haven't EU rules on farm fertiliser stopped the Baltic Sea from being choked by algae?
answer: The directive's limited success in the Baltic Sea illustrates a fundamental design flaw: the directive operates through national Nitrate Vulnerable Zones, but the Baltic catchment area spans nine countries — including non-EU states Russia and Belarus. According to the Baltic Marine Environment Protection Commission (HELCOM)'s 2021 "State of the Baltic Sea" report, agricultural nitrogen inputs into the Baltic have decreased by only 12% since 2000, far short of the 50% reduction target. The EEA's 2022 report identifies two additional reasons: the directive does not limit phosphorus — which is often the limiting nutrient in freshwater systems — and it covers only agriculture, not municipal wastewater or industrial sources. The 2016 EU Nutrient Action Plan (COM(2016) 929) attempted to address these gaps but lacked binding targets. In the end, the Nitrates Directive is a supply-side measure: it restricts what farmers can spread, but does not require them to reduce livestock numbers or improve manure processing technologies.
title: Council Directive 91/676/EEC concerning the protection of waters against pollution caused by nitrates from agricultural sources

question: Why did Europe’s main law protecting wildlife and natural areas survive attempts to scrap it in the 2010s?
answer: The 2014-2016 "Fitness Check" — a comprehensive evaluation requested by Commission President Jean-Claude Juncker amid pressure from agriculture and development lobbies — found that the directive's objectives remained valid. Environmental NGOs (WWF, BirdLife, Friends of the Earth) mounted a massive campaign, collecting over 520,000 public responses to the consultation, of which 96% supported retaining the directives. The European Parliament's Environment Committee vote in 2016 against reopening the legislation effectively killed the revision attempt. The scientific evidence — the EEA's 2015 State of Nature report showing continued biodiversity decline — argued against weakening protections.
title: Council Directive 92/43/EEC on the conservation of natural habitats and of wild fauna and flora

question: Why is the rule that forces developers to check if a project harms protected habitats so controversial?
answer: Article 6(3) requires any plan or project likely to have a "significant effect" on a Natura 2000 site to undergo assessment before approval. Developers and national governments argue this creates costly delays and blocks essential infrastructure. The 2007 *Waddenzee* ruling expanded its scope to cover cumulative impacts of multiple projects. The Commission estimated in 2016 that assessments take 6-18 months on average, and 67% of projects assessed received approval with conditions, 22% required significant modifications, and 11% were rejected outright — figures from the Commission's 2016 evaluation.
title: Council Directive 92/43/EEC on the conservation of natural habitats and of wild fauna and flora

question: How do EU rules protecting natural habitats work together with the separate rules protecting wild birds?
answer: The two directives function as a single legal framework — the "Nature Directives" — with the Habitats Directive extending site and species protection to non-bird species and habitats. Birds remain protected under the 1979 Birds Directive. Both directives use the same Natura 2000 network of sites. In 2007, the Commission merged their reporting requirements into a single Article 17 reporting cycle. The 2016 Fitness Check assessed both together, concluding they were "fit for purpose" but required better implementation.
title: Council Directive 92/43/EEC on the conservation of natural habitats and of wild fauna and flora

question: Can a 14-year-old get a part-time job in Europe?
answer: Yes, but only light work — such as babysitting, newspaper delivery, or light shop work — and only outside school hours and with a permit in some countries. The directive allows children aged 13–14 to do light work, and member states may set a higher minimum (e.g., 14 in some). The key restriction is that the work must not harm their health, safety, or education.
title: Council Directive 94/33/EC on the protection of young people at work

question: What kinds of work are banned for teenagers under EU law?
answer: Teenagers under 18 are banned from night work (generally 10 p.m. to 6 a.m.), from working more than 8 hours a day or 40 hours a week, and from any job involving dangerous machinery, toxic chemicals, extreme temperatures, or physical strain beyond their capacity. Specific lists of prohibited activities vary by country but cover sectors like mining, demolition, and slaughterhouses.
title: Council Directive 94/33/EC on the protection of young people at work

question: Do EU rules apply to all children, even those working in family businesses?
answer: The directive contains an important exemption: member states may exclude work in family undertakings (e.g., a child helping in a family farm or shop) provided it is not harmful or dangerous. Many countries, such as Italy and Spain, have used this exemption. However, the exemption does not apply to industrial work or work that exceeds light duties. In practice, family exemptions have been criticised for making enforcement difficult in agriculture and small retail.
title: Council Directive 94/33/EC on the protection of young people at work

question: Why does European law allow 13-year-olds to work at all, if the minimum school-leaving age is often higher?
answer: The directive reflects a political compromise between countries like the UK and Germany, which already permitted light work from age 13, and countries like Belgium, where the minimum was 15 for any paid work. The 13–14 light work category was seen as a way to preserve cultural traditions (e.g., summer berry picking, newspaper rounds) without forcing children into the adult labour market. The condition is strictly that the work must not interfere with education.
title: Council Directive 94/33/EC on the protection of young people at work

question: Why do EU rules on part-time work only cover part-time jobs, not temporary or agency work?
answer: The directive was part of a trilogy of atypical work measures, but the social partners failed to agree on temporary and agency work in the 1996 negotiations. The Commission later adopted separate directives: the Fixed-term Work Directive (1999/70/EC) in 1999, and the Temporary Agency Work Directive (2008/104/EC) in 2008. The part-time agreement was the easiest to achieve because employers saw part-time work as less disruptive to business models than agency or fixed-term contracts.
title: Council Directive 97/81/EC concerning the Framework Agreement on part-time work concluded by UNICE, CEEP and the ETUC

question: Why did the UK oppose EU part-time work rules under John Major but support them under Tony Blair?
answer: John Major's Conservative government regarded the Maastricht Social Protocol as a "socialist plot" to impose EU-level employment costs on British business. Major negotiated the UK's opt-out in 1991 and maintained it. The Labour Party, under Blair's "New Labour" agenda, had committed to signing the Social Chapter in its 1997 manifesto. Blair signed the Social Chapter at the Amsterdam European Council in June 1997, making UK adoption of the directive a formality.
title: Council Directive 97/81/EC concerning the Framework Agreement on part-time work concluded by UNICE, CEEP and the ETUC

question: Why did employers agree to negotiate EU rules on part-time work in the first place?
answer: UNICE agreed to negotiate because the alternative — a Commission-drafted directive — would likely have been more prescriptive and less favourable. By negotiating the Framework Agreement, employers secured a more flexible text that allowed objective justifications for differential treatment and did not create a general right to move from full-time to part-time work. A leaked UNICE internal memorandum from 1996, cited by the ETUC, reportedly stated: "Our best outcome is a weak framework agreement that pre-empts a strong directive."
title: Council Directive 97/81/EC concerning the Framework Agreement on part-time work concluded by UNICE, CEEP and the ETUC

question: How did EU part-time work rules affect women specifically?
answer: Overwhelmingly positively. In 1996, women made up 80% of EU part-time workers, according to Eurostat. The directive eliminated explicit pay discrimination and gave women the right to equal treatment on a pro-rata basis for pensions, training, and promotion. The European Women's Lobby, in its 1998 evaluation, described the directive as "the single most important EU legal instrument for gender equality in the labour market since the Equal Pay Directive."
title: Council Directive 97/81/EC concerning the Framework Agreement on part-time work concluded by UNICE, CEEP and the ETUC

question: Why do EU rules on mass layoffs let each country set its own thresholds for when they apply?
answer: The directive sets minimum EU thresholds but allows member states to implement stricter rules because labour market structures vary enormously. The 1998 text included a "non-regression clause" to ensure that countries like France (which required consultation for any 10+ dismissals) could keep their higher standards. The compromise was brokered to avoid a one-size-fits-all formula that would have broken existing social models.
title: Council Directive 98/59/EC on the approximation of the laws of the Member States relating to collective redundancies

question: Why don't some EU countries protect public sector workers under the same mass layoff rules as private sector workers?
answer: Article 1(3) allows member states to exclude workers in public administration, a loophole originally inserted to accommodate German and French civil service traditions where employment is governed by public law, not private contracts. The 2018 Commission study found that 12 member states have used this exclusion, leaving an estimated 2.5 million public sector workers outside the directive's scope — a situation the European Parliament's Employment Committee has repeatedly criticised.
title: Council Directive 98/59/EC on the approximation of the laws of the Member States relating to collective redundancies

question: Why isn't there a single EU agency that enforces the rules on mass layoffs across all countries?
answer: The directive relies on national enforcement because the EU Treaty — even after Amsterdam — does not grant the Commission direct investigatory powers over individual employer decisions. The 1998 text deliberately avoided creating a European-level supervisory agency, partly due to subsidiarity concerns and partly because member states (led by the UK and Germany) insisted that labour law remained a national competence. The Commission's role is limited to infringement proceedings against member states that fail to properly transpose the directive.
title: Council Directive 98/59/EC on the approximation of the laws of the Member States relating to collective redundancies

question: Why is it called "DAC6" instead of a boring official number?
answer: The "DAC" acronym refers to the Directive on Administrative Cooperation (2011/16/EU), which is the parent framework for tax information exchange between EU member states. Each amendment is numbered sequentially: DAC1 covered automatic exchange of financial account information (based on the Common Reporting Standard), DAC2 dealt with tax rulings, DAC3 with country-by-country reporting, DAC4 with access to anti-money-laundering information, DAC5 with beneficial ownership, and DAC6 with mandatory disclosure of aggressive tax arrangements. The numbering reflects its place as the sixth addition to this growing framework.
title: Council Directive (EU) 2018/822 amending Directive 2011/16/EU as regards mandatory automatic exchange of information in the field of taxation in relation to reportable cross-border arrangements

question: Who exactly counts as a "middleman" who has to report tax schemes?
answer: An intermediary is any person who designs, markets, organises, makes available, or implements a reportable cross-border arrangement, or who provides aid, assistance, or advice with respect to it. This explicitly includes tax advisors, accountants, lawyers, notaries, banks, insurance companies, and consultants. The directive also extends to any person who, given their involvement in the arrangement, could reasonably be expected to know that it is reportable. If a corporate group has an in-house tax department designing a cross-border financing structure, the department itself becomes the intermediary. The broad scope was deliberate — the OECD's 2015 BEPS Action 12 report noted that "limiting the definition to only those who are formally appointed or contracted"
title: Council Directive (EU) 2018/822 amending Directive 2011/16/EU as regards mandatory automatic exchange of information in the field of taxation in relation to reportable cross-border arrangements

question: Why do lawyers, accountants, and advisors have to report tax deals instead of just the people paying tax?
answer: Intermediaries are the gatekeepers of tax planning schemes — they possess the technical knowledge and documentation to know immediately what is reportable, and leaking shows they were the key enablers. Taxpayers, particularly individuals or small businesses, often lack the expertise to identify aggressive arrangements. The OECD's BEPS Action 12 report argued that targeting intermediaries "makes the reporting system more effective" because they operate at scale and can be held to professional standards. Additionally, intermediaries are easier to monitor: they must register with professional bodies, making non-compliance more detectable. Had DAC6 only targeted taxpayers, the reporting rate would likely have been far lower — the Commission's 2018 impact assessment estimated that taxpayer-only reporting would capture only 20-30% of aggressive arrangements versus 70-80% with intermediary reporting.
title: Council Directive (EU) 2018/822 amending Directive 2011/16/EU as regards mandatory automatic exchange of information in the field of taxation in relation to reportable cross-border arrangements

question: Do these reporting rules apply to tax plans that are legal and not meant to cheat?
answer: Yes, critically. The directive covers "aggressive tax planning" — arrangements that, while technically legal, exploit mismatches between tax systems, preferential regimes, or other features to achieve a tax advantage inconsistent with the intent of the law. For example, a classic "hybrid mismatch" arrangement (where the same payment is treated as a deduction in one country and not income in another) is reportable even if fully compliant with national law. This "grey area" approach was intentional: the European Parliament's resolution of 6 July 2016, following the Panama Papers, stated that transparency should extend to "legal but abusive" structures. The consequence is that tax advisors must now report schemes they might once have defended as compliant, forcing a fundamental shift in professional ethics.
title: Council Directive (EU) 2018/822 amending Directive 2011/16/EU as regards mandatory automatic exchange of information in the field of taxation in relation to reportable cross-border arrangements

question: Why did the EU need its own law when the OECD already had a global deal on minimum corporate taxes?
answer: The OECD agreement is a political commitment, not legally binding. To enforce the global minimum tax, each jurisdiction needs domestic law. The EU directive provides a uniform, enforceable legal framework across all 27 member states, ensuring consistent implementation and preventing member states from undercutting each other. It also avoids the "gold-plating" problem where countries implement rules differently, which could undermine the policy's effectiveness.
title: Council Directive (EU) 2022/2523 on ensuring a global minimum level of taxation for multinational enterprise groups and large-scale domestic groups in the Union

question: Why did Ireland, Luxembourg, and the Netherlands eventually agree to the EU's minimum corporate tax rules?
answer: These countries had little choice. By 2021, the OECD had built a consensus of 137 countries—including the US, China, and India—that made continued opposition diplomatically and economically unsustainable. Moreover, the directive's Qualified Domestic Minimum Top-up Tax (QDMTT) allowed these countries to collect the top-up tax themselves rather than see it go to other jurisdictions, partially preserving their tax base. Ireland's Finance Minister Paschal Donohoe acknowledged that "the global consensus is now such that Ireland's 12.5% rate is no longer a sustainable position" in July 2021.
title: Council Directive (EU) 2022/2523 on ensuring a global minimum level of taxation for multinational enterprise groups and large-scale domestic groups in the Union

question: Why is the global minimum corporate tax rate set at 15%?
answer: The 15% rate emerged from negotiations within the OECD/G20 Inclusive Framework, balancing competing demands. High-tax countries like France and Germany wanted a higher rate (20% or more) to increase revenue and reduce the incentive for profit shifting. Low-tax countries like Ireland argued for lower rates (12.5% or below). The OECD's 2021 economic impact assessment estimated that 15% would capture about 80% of the profit shifting benefits, while a higher rate risked irreparably damaging small low-tax countries. The rate also aligned with the US's proposed 15% minimum in the Biden administration's Made in America Tax Plan.
title: Council Directive (EU) 2022/2523 on ensuring a global minimum level of taxation for multinational enterprise groups and large-scale domestic groups in the Union

question: Why did the 9/11 attacks finally make EU countries agree to fast-track extradition for suspects?
answer: The 9/11 attacks broke the political deadlock by providing a security-driven justification for sacrificing national sovereignty in extradition. The special European Council summit on 21 September 2001 created a collective decision-making framework where Member States found it politically untenable to oppose anti-terrorism measures. The Spanish presidency turned the EAW into a flagship initiative, and the Commission drafted the text in two months — an impossible speed before the attacks.
title: Council Framework Decision on the European Arrest Warrant and the Surrender Procedures between Member States

question: Why does the EU skip the usual "is it a crime in both countries" check for 32 serious offences like terrorism or fraud?
answer: The list of 32 serious offences — including terrorism, murder, and drug trafficking — was designed to ensure that no Member State could refuse surrender simply because its national law defined the offence differently. The logic was that these offences were considered serious crimes across all Member States, making the extra requirement of dual criminality an unnecessary bureaucratic hurdle. The European Court of Justice upheld this approach in 2007 (C-303/05), ruling that the list was sufficiently clear and did not violate legal certainty.
title: Council Framework Decision on the European Arrest Warrant and the Surrender Procedures between Member States

question: Why did EU countries decide to just accept each other's arrest warrants instead of making their criminal laws the same?
answer: Mutual recognition was the politically feasible option. Harmonising criminal codes across 15 (now 27) legal traditions — ranging from common law to civil law, from inquisitorial to adversarial systems — would have required treaty changes and taken years. Mutual recognition allowed Member States to keep their own criminal procedures while agreeing to treat another Member State's judicial decision as equivalent to their own. This borrowed directly from the single market's "Cassis de Dijon" principle, which had already proven effective in goods trade.
title: Council Framework Decision on the European Arrest Warrant and the Surrender Procedures between Member States

question: Why did the UK almost pull out of the EU's fast-track extradition system?
answer: The UK's 2014 crisis was driven by two factors: first, a series of high-profile cases where EAWs were used for minor offences (such as a man surrendered for failing to pay a £12 council tax surcharge), which the tabloid press framed as EU overreach. Second, the UK's opt-out protocol from the Lisbon Treaty gave it a political opportunity to renegotiate its justice and home affairs relationship. Home Secretary Theresa May used the threat of opting out to secure additional safeguards, including a requirement for proportionality checks by the issuing state. In fact, the UK opted back into 35 measures including the EAW, recognising the system's operational value.
title: Council Framework Decision on the European Arrest Warrant and the Surrender Procedures between Member States

question: Why did EU fishing rules require satellite tracking for all boats longer than 12 metres?
answer: The 2006 Court of Auditors report found that paper logbooks were routinely falsified — with Spanish vessels reporting catches from protected areas as coming from outside them, and Irish vessels claiming to have fished in open water when they were actually inside 12-nautical-mile limits. Satellite VMS provided verifiable, real-time data that could be cross-checked against catch reports, making it nearly impossible to claim a false position. The 12-metre threshold was a compromise: full VMS for the 15,000 largest EU vessels that account for 80% of total catch volume, while exempting the 80,000 smaller vessels that caught the remaining 20%.
title: Council Regulation (EC) No 1224/2009 establishing a Community control system for ensuring compliance with the rules of the common fisheries policy

question: Why did it take so long for the EU to pass new fishing enforcement rules, even during a crisis?
answer: The fishing industry's political power in southern member states (Spain, Italy, France, Greece) meant that any reform that threatened small-scale fishing families faced fierce resistance. Spain's fisheries minister Elena Espinosa personally lobbied Commissioner Borg in 2008 to drop the electronic logbook requirement, arguing it would destroy the artisanal fleet. The regulation also required a complete re-engineering of national fisheries databases — many member states had no computerised system at all. The three-year implementation delay (2009-2012) was built in precisely to allow Italy, Greece, and Portugal to build their IT infrastructure, which the Commission co-funded through the European Fisheries Fund.
title: Council Regulation (EC) No 1224/2009 establishing a Community control system for ensuring compliance with the rules of the common fisheries policy

question: How do EU fishing control rules connect to the rules against illegal, unreported, and unregulated fishing?
answer: The Illegal, Unreported and Unregulated (IUU) Fishing Regulation (1005/2008/EC) was adopted one year earlier, targeting third-country vessels and trade. The Control Regulation complements it by focusing on EU-flagged vessels and domestic enforcement. Together they form a "control package" that the Commission explicitly described as "two sides of the same coin." The IUU Regulation created the "catch certification scheme" requiring imported fish to prove legal origin; the Control Regulation ensures that EU-caught fish can meet the same traceability standard, preventing EU fleets from being undercut by illegal imports.
title: Council Regulation (EC) No 1224/2009 establishing a Community control system for ensuring compliance with the rules of the common fisheries policy

question: Why didn't EU merger rules just keep using the old 'dominance' test?
answer: The old dominance test prevented intervention against anti-competitive effects in oligopolistic markets that lacked a single dominant player. In economics-laden language, the Commission's 2001 Green Paper argued that the dominance test was "ill-suited to deal with oligopolistic market structures where collective dominance could be established without any single firm holding a dominant position." The SIEC test allowed the Commission to block mergers that would significantly impede competition even if no single firm would hold over 40% market share — for example, when merging two smaller firms in a four-player market would reduce competitive pressure.
title: Council Regulation (EC) No 139/2004 on the control of concentrations between undertakings (the EC Merger Regulation)

question: Why did those 2002 court rulings change how the EU reviews big company mergers?
answer: These rulings were not just legal losses — they were institutional humiliations. The court found the Commission had engaged in "manifest errors of assessment" and had "adduced insufficient evidence" to meet the legal standard of proof. In the Airtours case, the court annulled the Commission's finding that the travel industry would see coordinated effects, saying the Commission had "failed to establish to the requisite legal standard" that coordination was likely. This forced Commissioner Monti to overhaul the entire merger review process, including creating a Chief Economist position in DG Competition (filled by Lars-Hendrik Röller in 2003) and strengthening the internal peer review system to ensure economic evidence met court standards.
title: Council Regulation (EC) No 139/2004 on the control of concentrations between undertakings (the EC Merger Regulation)

question: Why was 2004 the right time to update how Europe approves or blocks corporate takeovers?
answer: Two factors converged: enlargement and the Monti crisis. The EU was to expand from 15 to 25 members on 1 May 2004. Each new member had its own merger control regime, creating fragmentation the Commission argued would cost businesses billions in legal fees and delays. Simultaneously, Monti needed a legislative win to restore DG Competition's credibility after the 2002 court losses. The Irish Council Presidency (January-June 2004) made merger control reform a priority, and the European Parliament was eager to finalise the reform before the European elections in June 2004. The regulation entered into force on the same day as enlargement — 1 May 2004 — symbolically linking the two.
title: Council Regulation (EC) No 139/2004 on the control of concentrations between undertakings (the EC Merger Regulation)

question: What is a European Cooperative Society and who can create one?
answer: It is a legal form that allows a cooperative—a business owned and run by its members—to be registered under EU law rather than any single country’s law. To create one, you need at least five natural persons or two legal entities (like existing cooperatives) from at least two different EU countries. The cooperative must have a minimum share capital of €30,000 and follow democratic decision-making (one member, one vote).
title: Council Regulation (EC) No 1435/2003 of 22 July 2003 on the Statute for a European Cooperative Society (SCE)

question: Why are there so few European Cooperative Societies in existence?
answer: The main reasons are the high minimum capital (€30,000), the two-country requirement, and the fact that an SCE still pays taxes according to each national system. Many small cooperatives cannot afford the capital, and large ones see little advantage over national forms. The Commission’s 2018 evaluation found only about 300 SCEs across the EU, mostly in France and Italy.
title: Council Regulation (EC) No 1435/2003 of 22 July 2003 on the Statute for a European Cooperative Society (SCE)

question: How does a European Cooperative Society differ from a European Company (SE)?
answer: The SE is for ordinary limited companies; the SCE is specifically for cooperatives. Unlike an SE, an SCE must follow cooperative principles—profits are shared proportionally to members’ transactions, not capital, and decisions are made on a one-member-one-vote basis. The SE also has a higher minimum capital (€120,000) and no requirement to have members in two countries.
title: Council Regulation (EC) No 1435/2003 of 22 July 2003 on the Statute for a European Cooperative Society (SCE)

question: Can an existing national cooperative convert into a European Cooperative Society?
answer: Yes, but only if it has members or subsidiaries in at least two EU countries. A purely domestic cooperative cannot convert. This rule was intended to prevent “forum shopping” but in practice it excluded the very cooperatives that might have benefited most.
title: Council Regulation (EC) No 1435/2003 of 22 July 2003 on the Statute for a European Cooperative Society (SCE)

question: Why didn't a European Company have to be publicly traded on a stock exchange?
answer: The regulation deliberately allowed both listed and unlisted companies to form an SE, because the original aim was to facilitate cross-border operations for all types of firms, not just those raising capital on public markets. However, in practice, unlisted SEs became the dominant form (over 90% by 2019, per Commission data), as many were small entities using the SE label for internal restructuring or for a single cross-border merger, rather than for public trading. The lack of a listing requirement also reduced scrutiny of these entities, contributing to the "brass plate" problem.
title: Council Regulation (EC) No 2157/2001 on the Statute for a European Company (SE)

question: Why did EU rules for creating a European company fail to stop companies from shopping for the most lenient national laws?
answer: The Delaware phenomenon in the US — where companies incorporate in a single state with favourable laws — did not replicate in the EU because SE registration remained tied to the national law of the member state where the SE's registered office and head office are located. This "real seat" requirement, upheld in the 2007 *Cartesio* ruling (Case C-210/06), prevented SEs from being registered in a low-regulation state without transferring their management. The Commission’s 2019 evaluation noted that this limitation, combined with harmonised company law in many areas, meant the SE never offered the same flexibility as Delaware. Firms seeking light regulation simply converted to national forms in jurisdictions like Luxembourg or the UK.
title: Council Regulation (EC) No 2157/2001 on the Statute for a European Company (SE)

question: Who fought hardest against the creation of a European company law and why?
answer: German trade unions and the German government under Chancellor Gerhard Schröder (SPD) were the most powerful opponents during the 1990s, because they feared the SE form would allow large corporations like Volkswagen or Siemens to bypass the 1976 *Mitbestimmungsgesetz* (Co-determination Act), which mandates a 50% worker representation on supervisory boards for companies with over 2,000 employees. The compromise — Directive 2001/86/EC requiring negotiated worker participation — was the price Germany demanded. French government negotiators, led by Finance Minister Laurent Fabius, also demanded safeguards to prevent SEs from evading French social law, such as the *Code du Travail*’s works councils. The eventual vote in favour was brokered by the rotating Portuguese Council Presidency in late 2001, with Germany voting in favour only after the directive was secured.
title: Council Regulation (EC) No 2157/2001 on the Statute for a European Company (SE)

question: Why did the EU create such detailed rules for organic farming?
answer: The core reason was trust. The 2003 European Court of Auditors report showed that national control systems were failing, with consumers at risk of buying mislabelled products. Without harmonised EU rules, the organic label — which commands a price premium — became vulnerable to free-riding and fraud. Regulation 834/2007 was designed to make the label credible across all 27 member states.
title: Council Regulation (EC) No 834/2007 on Organic Production and Labelling of Organic Products

question: Why are genetically modified organisms (GMOs) banned so strictly in EU organic food?
answer: The prohibition reflected both the organic sector’s founding philosophy and intense consumer pressure. In the 1990s, the emergence of genetically modified crops raised alarm among organic farmers, who feared contamination of their fields through cross-pollination. The European Parliament during the 2004-2009 term, led by the Greens, successfully pushed for a zero-tolerance approach on GMO presence in organic products, despite opposition from the biotechnology industry represented by EuropaBio. The final text prohibits all GMO use, with a limit of 0.9% for accidental presence — the same threshold used for mandatory labelling of GM content in conventional food.
title: Council Regulation (EC) No 834/2007 on Organic Production and Labelling of Organic Products

question: Why did it take until 2007 for the EU to pass a unified organic food law, even though earlier rules existed?
answer: Earlier attempts to update Regulation 2092/91/EEC had failed because the conventional farming lobby effectively blocked reform in the 1990s, arguing that the market was small enough to manage nationally. The shift came when the organic sector grew sufficiently large — with retail sales doubling between 2000 and 2005 — that the cost of fragmentation became unbearable. The German wine scandal of 2005 was the political lightning rod, proving that even wealthy member states could not enforce their own rules effectively.
title: Council Regulation (EC) No 834/2007 on Organic Production and Labelling of Organic Products

question: Do EU organic food rules also apply to products imported from outside Europe?
answer: Yes, but with significant complexity. Under Regulation 834/2007, imports could enter the EU organic market through two pathways: equivalence (where the exporting country’s rules were judged equivalent, not identical) or by direct compliance with EU rules. This created tensions with trading partners like the US, where the National Organic Program allowed different practices. The 2018 revision (Regulation 2018/848) moved toward a stricter ‘compliance’ model, requiring imported products to be produced under EU-level rules by 2026, a change that sparked protests from developing country exporters.
title: Council Regulation (EC) No 834/2007 on Organic Production and Labelling of Organic Products

question: Does the EU have a prosecutor who can arrest people across borders?
answer: Yes. The European Public Prosecutor's Office (EPPO) can issue European arrest warrants and request asset freezes across all 22 participating member states. It can order seizures of property, freeze bank accounts, and demand documents — but it cannot conduct arrests directly; national police execute its warrants. It started work in June 2021 and by 2023 was handling over 1,900 cases.
title: Council Regulation (EU) 2017/1939 implementing enhanced cooperation on the establishment of the European Public Prosecutor’s Office (‘the EPPO’)

question: Why can’t national police handle EU fraud on their own?
answer: They often cannot, because the fraud is cross-border. A VAT carousel scheme might involve companies in five different EU countries, with the stolen money moving through banks in a sixth. National prosecutors lack jurisdiction outside their own country and often lack the resources to coordinate complex multi-country investigations. The European Commission’s own statistics showed that before the EPPO, member states recovered only about 18% of the money stolen from the EU budget between 2012 and 2016 — the rest was lost.
title: Council Regulation (EU) 2017/1939 implementing enhanced cooperation on the establishment of the European Public Prosecutor’s Office (‘the EPPO’)

question: Is every EU country part of the European cross-border fraud prosecutor's office?
answer: No. Only 22 of the 27 EU member states participate. Poland, Hungary, Sweden, Denmark, and Ireland opted out, though for very different reasons: Poland and Hungary opposed it on sovereignty grounds as their rule-of-law disputes with the Commission worsened; Sweden and Denmark have constitutional concerns about transferring criminal jurisdiction to an EU body; Ireland, like Denmark, has an opt-out from justice and home affairs cooperation. Participating and non-participating states still cooperate through Eurojust.
title: Council Regulation (EU) 2017/1939 implementing enhanced cooperation on the establishment of the European Public Prosecutor’s Office (‘the EPPO’)

question: What happens if a parent takes a child to another EU country without permission?
answer: If a child is wrongfully removed or retained in another EU member state, the left-behind parent can apply for a return order under the 1980 Hague Convention, but with EU-specific enhancements: a specialised court must handle the case, and it must issue a first-instance decision within six weeks. The child’s views must be heard unless it would harm them. If the court orders return, the decision is automatically enforceable across all member states without any extra procedure – no *exequatur* needed.
title: Council Regulation (EU) 2019/1111 on jurisdiction, the recognition and enforcement of decisions in matrimonial matters and the matters of parental responsibility, and on international child abduction (recast)

question: How does EU law ensure children are heard in custody disputes across borders?
answer: The Brussels IIb regulation makes a child’s hearing mandatory in any proceeding concerning parental responsibility (including return cases), unless the court decides that a hearing would be “manifestly contrary” to the child’s best interests. The judge must give reasons if they deny a hearing. The regulation also encourages the use of “hearing the child via video” or through trained social workers to avoid physically transporting the child across borders.
title: Council Regulation (EU) 2019/1111 on jurisdiction, the recognition and enforcement of decisions in matrimonial matters and the matters of parental responsibility, and on international child abduction (recast)

question: Why did the EU update its child abduction rules?
answer: The previous rules (Brussels IIa, from 2003) caused persistent delays: some abduction cases took over a year to resolve, harming the child’s stability. The European Commission’s 2014 evaluation showed that many judges were not hearing children directly, and that parents had to go through two court procedures (first to get a return order, then to get it enforced in the other country). The recast collapsed these steps into one, introduced a strict six-week deadline, and made child-hearing the default – all to align with the child’s right to a prompt and fair process.
title: Council Regulation (EU) 2019/1111 on jurisdiction, the recognition and enforcement of decisions in matrimonial matters and the matters of parental responsibility, and on international child abduction (recast)

question: Can a court order about a child be enforced automatically in another EU country?
answer: Yes – for all parental responsibility decisions (custody, visitation, return orders), the regulation abolished *exequatur*. As of 1 August 2022, a judgment from one member state is directly enforceable in another without a separate declaration of enforceability. The parent simply presents a certified copy of the order, and the court in the enforcing state can only refuse if the order violates a fundamental principle of justice (such as a serious breach of the child’s right to be heard).
title: Council Regulation (EU) 2019/1111 on jurisdiction, the recognition and enforcement of decisions in matrimonial matters and the matters of parental responsibility, and on international child abduction (recast)

question: What makes a special bank bond safer than a regular bank bond?
answer: Covered bonds are secured by a separate pool of high-quality assets (usually mortgages or public loans), and investors have two claims: first against the cover pool, then against the issuing bank if the pool falls short. If the bank goes bankrupt, the cover pool is ring-fenced from other creditors, so investors can still recover their money. This dual-recourse structure made covered bonds the only bank debt that survived the 2008 financial crisis without a single default.
title: Covered Bond Directive and Regulation (2019/2162/EU and 2019/2160/EU)

question: Why did Denmark and Germany oppose a single EU rule for these safer bank bonds?
answer: Denmark and Germany have centuries-old covered bond systems with strict national rules — Denmark requires at least 20% over-collateralisation, while Germany's Pfandbrief Act imposes tight asset-quality standards. They feared that harmonisation would force them to lower their high standards to match less stringent systems in other countries, potentially destabilising their markets. The final directive addressed this by allowing member states to maintain stricter national rules on top of the EU minimum, preserving their higher protections.
title: Covered Bond Directive and Regulation (2019/2162/EU and 2019/2160/EU)

question: Do all EU countries now follow the same rules for these special bank bonds?
answer: No, the directive sets minimum standards only — countries can keep or impose stricter rules. For example, Denmark still requires 20% over-collateralisation (the EU minimum is 5%), and Germany continues to require that assets in the cover pool meet specific quality criteria not mandated by the directive. This creates a two-tier market: bonds that meet only the EU minimum get the EU label, while those meeting stricter national rules may carry additional quality certifications.
title: Covered Bond Directive and Regulation (2019/2162/EU and 2019/2160/EU)

question: What practical benefits does the official EU quality label give to investors?
answer: Bonds with the European Covered Bond label receive preferential regulatory treatment: they qualify for lower capital requirements (10% risk weight for senior tranches versus 20-50% for unsecured debt) and benefit from reduced large-exposure limits. This makes them cheaper for investors to hold, which in turn lowers the issuing bank's funding costs. The label also provides a harmonised disclosure template, making it easier for investors to compare bonds across countries.
title: Covered Bond Directive and Regulation (2019/2162/EU and 2019/2160/EU)

question: When can an EU country break normal asylum rules during a migrant crisis?
answer: A member state can declare a crisis and use faster, less protective asylum procedures only if a sudden, very large influx (or instrumentalisation by a third country) makes normal processing impossible. The country must first notify the European Commission and get a green light from the EU Council (by qualified majority). The crisis mode lasts up to six months, renewable once. During that time, the country can hold people in border zones for up to six months before granting a decision, apply stricter rules to families, and fast‑track deportations.
title: Crisis and Force Majeure Regulation (2024/1359/EU) — New Pact on Migration emergency provisions

question: Does the new EU migration system force countries to take in migrants from frontline states?
answer: No. The 2024 reform replaced mandatory relocation quotas with “flexible solidarity”. A member state hit by a crisis can request help, and other EU countries can choose among three options: relocate some asylum seekers, pay a financial contribution into an EU fund, or provide operational support (police, interpreters, equipment). The Council can later decide to make a specific form of solidarity mandatory (e.g., for minors), but only with a qualified majority vote. This compromise was the key that got the whole package through.
title: Crisis and Force Majeure Regulation (2024/1359/EU) — New Pact on Migration emergency provisions

question: What happened to the old Dublin system?
answer: The Dublin III Regulation (2013/604/EU) was effectively replaced by a new **Asylum and Migration Management Regulation (2024/1358/EU)**. The old rule that the first entry country handles the asylum application is preserved in principle, but a new “solidarity pool” mechanism allows the Commission to decide, after a crisis, that some applicants should be relocated to other member states even if not requested. The Crisis Regulation creates an even stronger override: during a crisis, the Commission can redistribute applicants without the member state’s consent if the Council agrees.
title: Crisis and Force Majeure Regulation (2024/1359/EU) — New Pact on Migration emergency provisions

question: Why did the Crisis Regulation take four years to pass?
answer: The main obstacle was mandatory relocation: frontline states wanted it; Visegrád states (Hungary, Poland, Czech Republic, Slovakia) blocked it. The breakthrough came in 2022 when Germany and France brokered a “flexible solidarity” alternative, and the Belarus border crisis showed that even anti‑relocation states could need EU help. The 2023 European Parliament elections also created a deadline: the rapporteurs knew that if the Pact was not voted on by April 2024, the entire reform would die and have to restart under a new Parliament. That urgency forced the final deal.
title: Crisis and Force Majeure Regulation (2024/1359/EU) — New Pact on Migration emergency provisions

question: Why does the EU want to limit how much of a key mineral any one country can process?
answer: The cap targets China's dominance in refining. China controls approximately 90% of rare earth processing, 60% of lithium refining, and 70% of cobalt refining. The 65% threshold was specifically chosen because no EU member state could realistically replace Chinese capacity alone, but the cap forces supply diversification to at least three sources. Without it, the Commission's 2023 impact assessment warned that by 2030, 80% of EU strategic raw material processing would remain in a single country.
title: Critical Raw Materials Act

question: Why did EU rules on essential minerals include recycling targets instead of just focusing on mining?
answer: Historically, EU recycling rates for critical raw materials were negligible — under 1% for rare earths and under 5% for lithium. The 25% recycling target for 2030 was the most contentious figure in negotiations, with mining companies arguing it was unachievable and environmental groups pushing for 40%. The final compromise reflects the reality that urban mining (from e-waste and battery scrap) is slower to scale than virgin extraction, but it also sends a signal to the circular economy industry that public procurement and state aid will prioritize recycled content.
title: Critical Raw Materials Act

question: Why did Europe finally agree on rules for securing key minerals after earlier plans fell through?
answer: Earlier EU raw materials initiatives — the 2008 Raw Materials Initiative and the 2020 European Raw Materials Alliance — were non-binding and under-resourced. The CRMA became binding because three factors converged in 2023: first, China's gallium/germanium controls provided a concrete, undeniable crisis. Second, the Inflation Reduction Act in the US created competitive pressure — European battery manufacturers threatened to relocate. Third, the Versailles Declaration in March 2022 had already committed EU leaders to "reduce strategic dependencies," so the CRMA fulfilled a political promise. The critical mass came when Germany shifted position after Chinese export controls, breaking the previous deadlock where Berlin had favored voluntary measures.
title: Critical Raw Materials Act

question: Why are environmental groups suing EU plans to boost mining and processing for green technology?
answer: The tension is between speed and ecological protection. The CRMA's fast-tracked permitting can override standard environmental impact assessments for "Strategic Projects" — the Commission's 2023 impact assessment acknowledged this could increase local habitat destruction. Environmental groups argue that mining for lithium and rare earths in Natura 2000 areas, which cover 18% of known EU mineral deposits, risks destroying the very ecosystems the green transition is supposed to protect. The European Environmental Bureau's 2024 legal analysis specifically targets the Regulation's Article 10, which allows Strategic Projects to bypass certain Habitats Directive procedures.
title: Critical Raw Materials Act

question: Can a company move its headquarters to another EU country without shutting down?
answer: Yes — that is exactly what the directive enables. A company registered in one Member State can convert into a company governed by the law of another Member State without winding up, liquidating, or re‑incorporating from scratch. It must follow a structured process: draft a conversion plan, have it verified by an independent expert, inform employees and creditors, hold a shareholder vote, and obtain a certificate from the departing state’s authority confirming the procedure was followed.
title: Cross-Border Conversions, Mergers and Divisions Directive

question: What protections exist for employees when a company relocates across Europe?
answer: The directive preserves employee participation rights — such as board‑level representation — when a company moves. If the departing company had a system of employee participation, the new company must offer equivalent rights to its workers. Employees and their representatives must be informed and consulted on the conversion plan at least one month before the shareholder meeting. These provisions were the red line of the European Parliament and trade unions during negotiations.
title: Cross-Border Conversions, Mergers and Divisions Directive

question: Why did European company mobility rules take so long to harmonise?
answer: Because Member States disagreed fundamentally on two issues: whether companies should be allowed to “shop” for a better legal regime, and how to protect workers’ rights. Earlier attempts, notably a 2007 Commission proposal for a 14th Company Law Directive on cross‑border seat transfers, died in Council. The breakthrough required a series of CJEU rulings that created legal rights without procedures, making legislative harmonisation unavoidable, and a political climate — post‑Brexit, post‑Panama Papers — in which transparency and social protection were harder to oppose.
title: Cross-Border Conversions, Mergers and Divisions Directive

question: Can abusive corporate moves to avoid taxes or worker rights be stopped?
answer: Yes. The directive includes a mandatory abuse‑prevention check. Before a company can leave, the competent authority of the departing Member State must assess whether the conversion serves “abusive or fraudulent purposes aimed at circumventing Union or national law.” This covers tax evasion, avoidance of employee participation, and creditor harm. Several Member States — including Germany and Poland — have built strict procedural screens for this check. A company that fails the test cannot convert; judicial review is available.
title: Cross-Border Conversions, Mergers and Divisions Directive

question: Why didn't the EU just set a maximum price for sending parcels between countries?
answer: The Commission explicitly considered price caps in its 2015 impact assessment, but rejected them for three reasons: they would require the EU to set rates in 24 official languages across 300+ intra-EU routes; they risked perverse outcomes (operators could raise domestic prices to compensate); and they would likely be overturned by the European Court of Justice as disproportionate. Instead, the Commission chose "transparency as a regulatory tool" — the theory being that if operators had to publish their tariffs, the market would police itself through consumer choice and competition. This approach had worked for airline fares under the 2008 Air Services Regulation, and the Commission hoped to replicate it for parcels.
title: Cross-Border Parcel Delivery Services Regulation

question: Why did the EU parcel rules focus on prices for regular people sending packages, not on prices for businesses?
answer: The regulation explicitly requires operators to publish only their "public tariffs" — the rates offered to occasional users (consumers and small businesses) — because that is where the largest disparities existed. The Commission's 2015 data showed that large e-commerce companies like Amazon and Zalando negotiated confidential discounts of up to 70% off public cross-border tariffs, meaning the hidden tariffs were actually competitive. The real problem was that SMEs and consumers, who lacked negotiating power, paid the full public rate. The regulation's trade-off was clear: public tariffs would be transparent, while business tariffs remained confidential to protect operators' commercial interests — a compromise that critics on the left, including MEP Lucy Anderson, argued "protects the very opacity that created the problem in the first place."
title: Cross-Border Parcel Delivery Services Regulation

question: Why did it take so long for the EU to agree on rules for cross-border parcel deliveries?
answer: The regulation spent over two years in the legislative process (proposed May 2016, adopted May 2018) — relatively fast for EU legislation, but slow for a Digital Single Market priority. The delay was caused by a standoff over the "reference tariff" provision. The Council of the EU, dominated by member states with large postal incumbents (France, Germany, the Netherlands), opposed requiring operators to submit confidential reference tariffs to regulators, arguing it would "chill commercial negotiations." The European Parliament insisted on the provision, led by MEP Anderson. The final compromise, brokered in December 2017 by the Maltese Presidency, allowed reference tariffs to be submitted only where a regulator had "reasonable grounds" to suspect an abuse — effectively a procedural hurdle that weakened the provision. Anderson called the compromise "a missed opportunity," but the Commission's 2022 review reported that regulators had used this power in 11 cases so far, suggesting the provision was not entirely neutered.
title: Cross-Border Parcel Delivery Services Regulation

question: Why was the EU's emergency fund for major cyberattacks set at €2.5 billion, not more?
answer: The €2.5 billion figure was a political compromise between Member States with high cybersecurity spending (the Nordic countries, Germany) who wanted a small fund to avoid moral hazard, and states with weaker capabilities (Southern and Eastern Europe) who wanted at least €5 billion. The Commission's 2024 impact assessment estimated that a major single cyberattack could cost the EU economy between €10 billion and €50 billion, but the fund was kept modest to ensure it supplemented, rather than replaced, national budgets.
title: Cyber Solidarity Act

question: How do the new EU rules for sharing cyber threat warnings work with the existing cybersecurity requirements for critical companies?
answer: NIS2 places incident reporting obligations on private operators in critical sectors; the CSA does not change those rules. Instead, the CSA focuses on public-sector coordination and financial support. The European Cybersecurity Incident Response Mechanism (EU-CyCLONe) created by the CSA operates as the operational arm of the **NIS Cooperation Group**, ensuring that the SOCs feed threat intelligence directly into NIS2's incident notification pipeline.
title: Cyber Solidarity Act

question: Do the EU's new cybersecurity cooperation rules apply to companies or governments outside Europe?
answer: The CSA is limited to EU Member States, but it allows for **associate participation by EEA countries** (Norway, Iceland, Liechtenstein) and **Switzerland** if they sign agreements to join the European SOC network. The fund is only accessible to EU Member States. Non-EU companies cannot directly apply for funding, but they can participate in threat-intelligence sharing through the SOCs if they operate critical infrastructure in the EU.
title: Cyber Solidarity Act

question: What happens if an EU country refuses to set up a national cyber threat monitoring center as required?
answer: The regulation requires each Member State to designate or establish a national SOC within six months of entry into force (by July 2025). If a Member State fails to do so, it cannot access the Cyber Solidarity Fund for either preparedness or emergency response. The Commission has the power to withhold Digital Europe Programme funding for cybersecurity projects until a SOC is designated. As of July 2025, two Member States had not designated SOCs; the Commission has issued formal notices to Hungary and Poland.
title: Cyber Solidarity Act

question: Who has to report income from a side hustle like selling on eBay or renting a room on Airbnb?
answer: DAC7 applies to any digital platform that facilitates the sale of goods (like eBay or Vinted), short-term rental of property (like Airbnb or Booking.com), personal services (like Uber or TaskRabbit), or vehicle rental. If you are a seller on such a platform in the EU and you have more than 30 transactions or earn more than €2,000 in a calendar year, the platform must report your income to your country's tax authority. This rule also applies to platforms based outside the EU if they have sellers within the EU.
title: DAC7 Directive on digital platform reporting obligations (2021/514/EU)

question: What happens if a platform doesn't follow the new EU reporting rules for online sellers?
answer: National tax authorities can impose significant penalties, including fines and business bans. For example, in France, non-compliant platforms face fines of up to €10,000 per seller not reported. In Germany, platforms can be blocked from operating entirely if they fail to report. The European Commission also has the power to launch infringement proceedings against member states that fail to properly implement the directive, as it did with Cyprus, Luxembourg, and Malta.
title: DAC7 Directive on digital platform reporting obligations (2021/514/EU)

question: Do the new EU reporting rules apply to non-European platforms that have European sellers?
answer: Yes. Any platform that connects EU-based sellers with buyers — regardless of where the platform is headquartered — must comply. This was a critical design feature: it prevents platforms from avoiding EU tax rules by relocating to places like the UK (post-Brexit), Switzerland, or the USA. Platforms must register in a single EU member state of their choice and report seller income to that state's tax authority, which then shares the data with other member states.
title: DAC7 Directive on digital platform reporting obligations (2021/514/EU)

question: Can a platform avoid reporting by claiming it's just a "marketplace" or "listing service"?
answer: No. The directive explicitly defines "platform" broadly to include any software that enables sellers to connect with users and facilitates a "relevant activity." The European Commission's 2023 explanatory notes clarified that even platforms that only provide a listing or matching service, and do not process payments, must still report. This closed a loophole that platforms like early-stage rental or freelancer marketplaces had sought to exploit.
title: DAC7 Directive on digital platform reporting obligations (2021/514/EU)

question: Why do new data-sharing rules exclude personal information?
answer: The Data Act is explicitly designed to complement the GDPR, not replace it. Personal data already has a regulatory framework under GDPR, which grants data subjects rights of access and portability. The Data Act focuses on the gap — non-personal data from IoT devices, such as machine performance metrics, soil composition readings, or industrial sensor logs — which had no clear legal framework for access and reuse. Where data is mixed (personal and non-personal), both regulations apply simultaneously, a complexity the Commission promised to address in guidance.
title: Data Act

question: Why did John Deere fight against rules that require sharing tractor data?
answer: John Deere's opposition reflected the broader agricultural equipment industry position. The company argued that revealing diagnostic data and maintenance protocols would expose proprietary algorithms and trade secrets — a claim the Commission's impact assessment acknowledged, but countered by noting that the Data Act's trade secrets provisions (Article 4) require data holders to protect trade secrets "by means of appropriate technical and organisational measures" before sharing. The practical effect: a manufacturer can refuse to share data if doing so would "likely cause serious damage" to trade secrets, but the burden of proof is on the manufacturer, not the data requester.
title: Data Act

question: Why are the rules that let you switch cloud providers easily delayed until 2027?
answer: The delayed application (12 January 2027) for cloud switching provisions reflects intense industry lobbying. Cloud providers argued that implementing full interoperability and data portability for enterprise workloads required significant infrastructure changes — particularly for "vertical" cloud services like software-as-a-service platforms. The Commission accepted a phased approach, with smaller providers receiving additional time. The European Cloud Federation, representing providers like OVHcloud and Deutsche Telekom, supported the provisions but warned that overly aggressive timelines could harm Europe's still-nascent cloud competitors.
title: Data Act

question: Why do data-sharing rules also cover automated contracts?
answer: The inclusion of smart contract provisions was a late addition during trilogue negotiations, driven by concerns about the reliability of automated data-sharing agreements. The regulation requires smart contracts used for data sharing to include a "kill switch" mechanism and to be auditable. This addresses cases like the 2022 collapse of algorithmic stablecoin TerraUSD, which raised broader questions about automated code without human oversight. The provisions apply only to smart contracts executing automated data-sharing agreements, not to blockchain applications generally.
title: Data Act
