The democratic trade-off was stark: limit the circulation of banned chemicals, or keep recycling rates high but risk POPs contaminating toys, furniture, and food packaging. Parliament chose the former.
title: Regulation (EU) 2019/1021 on Persistent Organic Pollutants (recast)

question: Why do some banned toxic chemicals still show up in everyday products?
answer: Nearly all POPs covered by the Regulation are prohibited from *new* production and use. However, products manufactured before the ban — so-called “legacy articles” — can remain in use (e.g., old furniture containing PBDEs, or buildings with PFOS-based coatings). The Regulation allows Member States to permit continued use until the product reaches end-of-life, provided no replacement exists. The consequence is that waste streams are the primary vector of exposure: as legacy products are discarded, their POPs content triggers strict destruction requirements. The Commission’s 2022 evaluation estimated that “legacy consumption” will be the dominant source of POPs waste until at least 2040, making waste management the core battlefield of POPs policy.
title: Regulation (EU) 2019/1021 on Persistent Organic Pollutants (recast)

question: What happens if a country doesn't enforce the EU's ban on long-lasting toxic pollutants?
answer: The Commission can initiate infringement proceedings under Article 258 TFEU. In 2021, the Commission sent a letter of formal notice to Greece for failing to ensure that POP-contaminated waste from old electronics was properly destroyed. In 2022, the CJEU ruled against Poland (Case C-563/20) for allowing the sale of building materials containing PCP (pentachlorophenol) above the 2019 limits. The Court imposed a fine of €10,000 per day until compliance. As of 2023, three other Member States (Bulgaria, Romania, and Lithuania) face pre-litigation cases over POP waste management failures.
title: Regulation (EU) 2019/1021 on Persistent Organic Pollutants (recast)

question: Why don't EU rules for online platforms like Amazon or Google Shopping set price limits or stop them from favoring their own products?
answer: The Juncker Commission chose procedural transparency over economic regulation because it was politically achievable. The Netherlands, Ireland, and Luxembourg would have vetoed any measure that forced Booking.com, Google, or Amazon to give competitors equal commercial terms. The P2B Regulation passed because it was weak enough not to trigger Member State opposition, leaving stronger measures to the later Digital Markets Act (2022).
title: Regulation (EU) 2019/1150 on promoting fairness and transparency for business users of online intermediation services (Platform-to-Business Regulation)

question: Why do small businesses still lose arguments with big platforms even after the EU passed rules to make disputes fairer?
answer: The regulation gives procedural rights but does not create a substantive right to a fair commercial outcome. A platform can still delist a seller for a legitimate reason — such as low customer ratings or intellectual property complaints — and the seller's only recourse is mediation, which is non-binding. The Commission's 2022 report found that mediation resolved only 24% of cases, with many businesses declining to participate due to fear of retaliation.
title: Regulation (EU) 2019/1150 on promoting fairness and transparency for business users of online intermediation services (Platform-to-Business Regulation)

question: How do the EU's rules for fair treatment of businesses on online platforms connect to the separate rules for the biggest tech companies (like the Digital Markets Act)?
answer: The P2B Regulation applies to all online intermediation services regardless of size, while the DMA (2022/1925/EU) imposes stricter obligations only on designated "gatekeepers" — the largest platforms. The DMA bans self-preferencing, data combing, and most-favoured-nation clauses — practices the P2B merely requires transparency about. The P2B is thus a baseline; the DMA is the enforcement upgrade.
title: Regulation (EU) 2019/1150 on promoting fairness and transparency for business users of online intermediation services (Platform-to-Business Regulation)

question: Why did the EU cap fees on a new type of cross-border pension at 1% when pension companies said it was too low?
answer: The 1% cap was a political compromise between consumer groups who wanted lower fees and industry who wanted none. According to the Commission's 2017 impact assessment, the average annual management fee for personal pensions across the EU was 0.9%, but this masked enormous variation: fees in some Member States exceeded 3% annually. The European Parliament's ECON committee initially proposed a 0.75% cap, while the Council proposed 1.5%. The final 1% figure emerged from trilogue negotiations in late 2018. Insurers warned that the cap would make PEPP unprofitable for smaller providers and certain customer segments, but the Commission and Parliament prioritized consumer protection after studies showed that even 1% annual fees could reduce total retirement savings by 20% over a 40-year career.
title: Regulation (EU) 2019/1238 on a Pan-European Personal Pension Product (PEPP)

question: Why hasn't the EU's plan for a single Europe-wide personal pension taken off?
answer: PEPP's failure to gain traction stems from a fundamental design flaw: the regulation created a product but did not harmonize the tax environment in which it operates. According to the European Commission's 2023 review, national tax rules effectively determine the product's attractiveness. In 22 of 27 Member States, PEPP contributions are not tax-deductible or are subject to less favorable treatment than national products. Additionally, the regulation left distribution and advice in national hands, meaning PEPP providers must still comply with 27 different advisory regimes and marketing rules. The product's portability, while theoretically valuable, applies only to the accumulation phase — payout structures remain subject to national rules, creating further friction.
title: Regulation (EU) 2019/1238 on a Pan-European Personal Pension Product (PEPP)

question: Why did Denmark and the Netherlands vote against the EU's new Europe-wide personal pension product?
answer: Both Denmark and the Netherlands already had mature, high-penetration occupational pension systems that cover 80-95% of their working populations. According to the European Commission's 2017 impact assessment, their existing systems offered fees well below the PEPP's 1% cap (average 0.3-0.5% annually in occupational plans). Dutch and Danish pension funds, represented by PensionsEurope, argued that a new EU product would not add value for their members but could undermine the efficiency of their collective systems by drawing assets away from low-cost, professionally managed funds. Their opposition reflected a broader tension in the PEPP negotiation between countries with well-developed private pension systems (Germany, Denmark, Netherlands, Sweden) and those with weaker coverage (particularly Southern and Eastern European Member States).
title: Regulation (EU) 2019/1238 on a Pan-European Personal Pension Product (PEPP)

question: Why did the EU create a standing armed border force instead of keeping the old temporary system?
answer: The 2015 migration crisis proved that member states could not be relied upon to protect the EU's external borders collectively. When Greece could not control its sea border, no other member state stepped in with sufficient resources. The Commission argued that the Schengen system required a "fully operational" border force, not a "pool of volunteers." The standing corps was designed to prevent a repeat of 2015, where unilateral border closures nearly destroyed Schengen.
title: Regulation (EU) 2019/1896 on the European Border and Coast Guard

question: Why do EU border rules include a way to complain about rights violations if those complaints are almost never accepted?
answer: The mechanism was a political compromise essential to secure the European Parliament's support, particularly from the S&D and Greens/EFA groups. Without it, the 2019 parliamentary vote would likely have failed. MEPs like Sophia in 't Veld demanded the mechanism after Frontex's involvement in pushback allegations in Greece and the Aegean Sea. The low acceptance rate has led to controversy: the European Ombudsman opened a strategic inquiry in 2021 into the mechanism's effectiveness, concluding in 2023 that it "lacked independence and effectiveness."
title: Regulation (EU) 2019/1896 on the European Border and Coast Guard

question: Why hasn't the EU reached its goal of having 10,000 permanent border guards ready to deploy?
answer: Multiple factors combined: national governments were reluctant to release their best border guards for EU service; fundamental rights vetting delayed recruitment; and the COVID-19 pandemic paused personnel deployments. The Commission's 2023 progress report also cited "insufficient financial incentives" — member states were paid only €16,000 per year per seconded officer, below many national salaries. The European Court of Auditors noted that the target was "politically driven, not operationally based."
title: Regulation (EU) 2019/1896 on the European Border and Coast Guard

question: Can the EU border agency actually overrule a country's own decisions about its borders?
answer: Theoretically, yes: under Article 42, if a member state's border controls are seriously deficient and it fails to comply with a vulnerability assessment recommendation, the Council can adopt a decision authorising Frontex to intervene. But this requires a qualified majority vote, and in practice has never been attempted. The provision's political unfeasibility has drawn criticism from the Parliament, with MEPs calling for a simpler trigger mechanism.
title: Regulation (EU) 2019/1896 on the European Border and Coast Guard

question: Can the EU block a foreign company from buying a European firm for security reasons?
answer: No, not directly. The regulation does not give the Commission a veto. Member states retain the final decision on each investment. But the regulation creates a cooperation mechanism where other member states and the Commission can raise objections and issue opinions. In practice, a negative opinion from the Commission often leads a member state to block or condition the deal, though it is not legally obliged to do so.
title: Regulation (EU) 2019/452 establishing a framework for the screening of foreign direct investments into the Union

question: Which sectors are covered by the FDI screening rules?
answer: The regulation lists critical infrastructure (energy, transport, water, health, communications, data storage); critical technologies (AI, robotics, semiconductors, dual-use goods); security of essential inputs (including food and energy); and access to sensitive information or media platforms. It also explicitly covers investments involving foreign state-owned enterprises, where the risk of state-directed acquisitions is considered higher.
title: Regulation (EU) 2019/452 establishing a framework for the screening of foreign direct investments into the Union

question: Why did the EU need a law if member states already screen investments?
answer: Before 2019, only about a third of EU member states had any screening mechanism. Even those that did had no obligation to tell each other about risky takeovers. This meant a Chinese investor acquiring a German robotics firm could also buy a French chipmaker without either country knowing the full picture. The regulation plugs that information gap and creates a floor of cooperation, while leaving member states free to keep stricter national rules.
title: Regulation (EU) 2019/452 establishing a framework for the screening of foreign direct investments into the Union

question: Has the regulation stopped any foreign takeovers?
answer: The regulation itself has not formally blocked a deal, but it has influenced outcomes. For example, in 2021, the Commission issued an opinion on a proposed acquisition of a Dutch semiconductor company by a Chinese entity; the Dutch government subsequently prohibited the deal under its own new screening law, citing the Commission’s analysis. More often, the regulation has led to conditions being imposed – such as requiring the acquirer to keep R&D in Europe – or to investors withdrawing early to avoid a public veto.
title: Regulation (EU) 2019/452 establishing a framework for the screening of foreign direct investments into the Union

question: Can any group of EU citizens force the European Commission to make a law?
answer: No. The ECI allows one million citizens to formally ask the Commission to consider a legislative proposal, but the Commission retains full discretion to decide whether to act. It can accept, partially accept, or reject the request, as long as it explains its reasoning within six months. The Commission has rejected more than half of successful initiatives — most controversially "One of Us" (2014) and "Stop Vivisection" (2015) — citing legal competence or scientific grounds.
title: Regulation (EU) 2019/788 on the European Citizens' Initiative

question: How hard is it to get a citizen petition approved compared to before 2020?
answer: Much easier under the 2019 regulation. The old system required organisers to use proprietary software that had to be individually certified by each member state — a process that took six months and cost up to €100,000 per campaign. The new centralised online system is free and reduces launch time to less than two weeks. Organisers also no longer need to collect passport numbers, only a copy of an identity document, which has doubled signature submission rates according to a 2021 Commission survey.
title: Regulation (EU) 2019/788 on the European Citizens' Initiative

question: Does the new EU petition system protect against fake signatures or fraud?
answer: Yes, but with trade-offs. National authorities must verify signatures within three months, checking against national identity databases for duplicate entries or ineligible signatories. However, the shift from requiring full passport numbers to scanned identity cards has made automated cross-border verification harder. The Commission's 2023 audit found that duplicate signatures ranged between 2-5% across member states, up from 1-3% under the old rules, but organisers say the simpler process is worth the slight increase in risk.
title: Regulation (EU) 2019/788 on the European Citizens' Initiative

question: Why did the EU need to pass a whole new set of rules for citizen petitions instead of just updating the old ones?
answer: Because the 2011 regulation was a legislative act under the ordinary legislative procedure, a full revision required a new regulation. The Commission considered a quick fix in 2015 but decided the problems were so systemic — including the failure of the proprietary software model and the administrative burden on member states — that a full repeal and replacement was justified. The 2019 regulation also added entirely new provisions, such as partial registration and a mandatory public hearing, which could not have been bolted onto the old text.
title: Regulation (EU) 2019/788 on the European Citizens' Initiative

question: Does the EU now have a permanent cybersecurity agency?
answer: Yes. Before the Cybersecurity Act, the European Union Agency for Cybersecurity (ENISA) was reauthorised every few years on a temporary basis. The Act made ENISA a permanent agency with a stable budget and expanded responsibilities, including managing the EU’s cybersecurity certification framework.
title: Regulation (EU) 2019/881 on ENISA (the European Union Agency for Cybersecurity) and on information and communications technology cybersecurity certification (Cybersecurity Act)

question: What does EU cybersecurity certification mean for products I buy?
answer: If a product—like a smart thermostat or a connected medical device—carries an EU cybersecurity certificate, it means the manufacturer has had it independently tested against a common set of security requirements valid in all 27 member states. The certification is voluntary for most products currently, but new laws will soon make it mandatory for many categories.
title: Regulation (EU) 2019/881 on ENISA (the European Union Agency for Cybersecurity) and on information and communications technology cybersecurity certification (Cybersecurity Act)

question: Why did the EU create a certification framework instead of just setting rules?
answer: The EU’s core problem before 2019 was not a lack of rules—it was a patchwork of incompatible national certification schemes. A product tested in Germany might need re‑testing in France. The Cybersecurity Act creates a single trusted certificate that works across Europe, removing that duplication. Over time, the framework also aims to raise the baseline security level for all certified products.
title: Regulation (EU) 2019/881 on ENISA (the European Union Agency for Cybersecurity) and on information and communications technology cybersecurity certification (Cybersecurity Act)

question: Why does the EU need its own official list of what counts as "green" instead of just trusting private eco-labels?
answer: Private green labels proliferated in the 2010s without a common standard, creating confusion and enabling greenwashing. A 2020 study by the European Securities and Markets Authority found that there were 68 different green bond frameworks globally, with no consistent criteria for what counted as "green." The EU's 2019 Behavioural Insights study showed that 89% of EU consumers considered it difficult to identify genuinely sustainable investments. The Taxonomy creates a single, legally enforceable standard that regulators can audit and that investors can rely on.
title: Regulation (EU) 2020/852 on the establishment of a framework to facilitate sustainable investment (EU Taxonomy Regulation)

question: Why was nuclear power officially labelled as a sustainable investment in Europe?
answer: Nuclear was included through the Complementary Climate Delegated Act (February 2022) after intensive lobbying by France, which generates 70% of its electricity from nuclear power, and by a coalition of central European countries with existing nuclear fleets. The Commission's own Joint Research Centre had concluded in 2021 that nuclear "does not cause more harm to human health or the environment than other electricity generation technologies" when lifecycle impacts are considered. Inclusion was opposed by Austria, Luxembourg, Germany, and environmental NGOs, who argued that nuclear creates long-term radioactive waste storage risks. The legal basis for inclusion is Article 10(2) of the Taxonomy Regulation, which allows the Commission to include activities that support the transition to climate neutrality, provided they do not lock in fossil-fuel assets.
title: Regulation (EU) 2020/852 on the establishment of a framework to facilitate sustainable investment (EU Taxonomy Regulation)

question: What happens to a company that says its activities are environmentally friendly under EU rules but actually isn't?
answer: The Taxonomy Regulation itself does not impose fines for incorrect self-declaration, but it creates liability through the broader EU regulatory framework. Article 23 of the Taxonomy Regulation requires Member States to establish effective penalties for misleading taxonomy-related claims. The European Securities and Markets Authority (ESMA) has issued enforcement priorities each year since 2022, and the 2023 coordinated enforcement action by national competent authorities resulted in 47 investigations into potential greenwashing by fund managers. Additionally, the Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (2022/2464/EU), which amends the Taxonomy's disclosure framework, requires limited assurance from statutory auditors on taxonomy-related data from 2024 and reasonable assurance from 2026. Fines under national transpositions vary: the German financial regulator BaFin can impose penalties of up to €5 million or 5% of annual turnover for misleading sustainability claims.
title: Regulation (EU) 2020/852 on the establishment of a framework to facilitate sustainable investment (EU Taxonomy Regulation)

question: How does the EU's official green investment list connect to the other rules companies must follow when reporting their environmental impact?
answer: The Taxonomy Regulation sits within a pyramid of linked legislation. At its base is the Sustainable Finance Disclosure Regulation (2019/2088/EU), which requires financial market participants to disclose how they integrate sustainability risks. The Taxonomy provides the definitional framework for those disclosures. Above it sits the Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (CSRD, 2022/2464/EU), which requires large companies to report according to European Sustainability Reporting Standards (ESRS) — and the ESRS explicitly reference Taxonomy-alignment data. At the apex is the European Green Deal, whose 2050 climate-neutrality target sets the overarching policy goal. The European Commission's 2024 review proposed a "simplification package" to harmonise reporting deadlines across these instruments, acknowledging that overlapping requirements had created compliance burdens.
title: Regulation (EU) 2020/852 on the establishment of a framework to facilitate sustainable investment (EU Taxonomy Regulation)

question: Does every coal region in the EU get money from this fund?
answer: No. Only regions that submit an approved Territorial Just Transition Plan are eligible. As of 2024, about 100 regions in 18 member states have received JTF allocations. Regions must demonstrate a clear path away from fossil fuels and invest in new economic activities – not simply keep old coal plants running.
title: Regulation (EU) 2021/1056 establishing the Just Transition Fund

question: Why did Poland threaten to block the EU's climate neutrality target?
answer: Because Poland had the most to lose. According to European Commission estimates, the Polish coal sector directly employed around 80,000 workers and indirectly supported hundreds of thousands more. Without a concrete financial safety net, the government argued that the 2050 climate neutrality goal would devastate its economy and trigger massive unemployment. The JTF was the price for Poland’s consent.
title: Regulation (EU) 2021/1056 establishing the Just Transition Fund

question: Can the fund be used to build new gas pipelines or power plants?
answer: Largely no. The original regulation allowed limited gas investments in exceptional circumstances for regions with no other fossil fuel alternative, but this loophole was tightened in the 2023 revision after environmental groups and the European Parliament protested. Since then, only renewable energy, district heating, and energy efficiency projects qualify for JTF support. Gas infrastructure is not financed.
title: Regulation (EU) 2021/1056 establishing the Just Transition Fund

question: How much money has actually been spent so far?
answer: The European Commission reported that by end-2023, about 15% of the total JTF allocation had been disbursed to member states, with the rest committed to approved projects but not yet paid out. The European Court of Auditors criticised the slow pace, noting that most regions had only completed the planning phase. The first concrete projects – reskilling programmes in Silesia, a hydrogen hub in Lusatia – are expected to start delivering tangible results from 2025 onward.
title: Regulation (EU) 2021/1056 establishing the Just Transition Fund

question: Why can EU countries lose funding if they break rule-of-law standards?
answer: The 2021-2027 CPR is the first cohesion regulation to incorporate a financial conditionality mechanism linked to the rule of law, via the separate Regulation 2020/2092. This means that if the Commission identifies systemic risks to the EU budget — for example, corruption in public procurement — it can suspend payments to the affected Member State. The mechanism was added after years of concerns raised by the European Parliament and net contributor states, following reports that cohesion funds were being misused in Hungary and Poland without consequence.
title: Regulation (EU) 2021/1060 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 24 June 2021 laying down common provisions on the European Regional Development Fund, the European Social Fund Plus, the Cohesion Fund, the Just Transition Fund and the European Maritime, Fisheries and Aquaculture Fund and financial rules for those and for the Asylum, Migration and Integration Fund, the Internal Security Fund and the Instrument for Financial Support for Border Management and Visa Policy

question: What changed in the rules for EU funding between 2014-2020 and now?
answer: The biggest change is the replacement of ex‑ante conditionality (which required Member States to have plans, but did not penalise non‑implementation) with binding "enabling conditions" that must be fulfilled continuously throughout the programming period. The rule‑book was also unified for eight funds, reducing the number of separate regulatory frameworks from four to one. Additionally, the new regulation requires that 30% of ERDF allocations go to climate‑related investments and 25% of ESF+ allocations to social inclusion, compared to the 2014-2020 period's broad targets that were often not met.
title: Regulation (EU) 2021/1060 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 24 June 2021 laying down common provisions on the European Regional Development Fund, the European Social Fund Plus, the Cohesion Fund, the Just Transition Fund and the European Maritime, Fisheries and Aquaculture Fund and financial rules for those and for the Asylum, Migration and Integration Fund, the Internal Security Fund and the Instrument for Financial Support for Border Management and Visa Policy

question: How do the new EU funding rules affect regions where I live?
answer: If your region receives cohesion funding, you are now subject to stricter monitoring of the partnership principle — local authorities, trade unions, and NGOs must be involved in programme design, or payments can be suspended. The Just Transition Fund specifically targets coal‑dependent regions; if your region is eligible (e.g., Silesia, Asturias, or the Ruhr), you will see economic diversification programmes funded by the JTF. For regions in Member States with rule-of-law concerns, payments may be delayed or frozen until conditions are met.
title: Regulation (EU) 2021/1060 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 24 June 2021 laying down common provisions on the European Regional Development Fund, the European Social Fund Plus, the Cohesion Fund, the Just Transition Fund and the European Maritime, Fisheries and Aquaculture Fund and financial rules for those and for the Asylum, Migration and Integration Fund, the Internal Security Fund and the Instrument for Financial Support for Border Management and Visa Policy

question: Does the EU now collect biometric data from children for visas?
answer: Yes. Regulation 2021/1134 lowered the minimum age for fingerprint and facial image collection from 12 to 6 years. The change was driven by security concerns that forged identity documents could be used for children, who were previously exempt. The European Data Protection Supervisor criticised the measure as disproportionate, but the Commission argued it was necessary to close a loophole exploited by organised crime.
title: Regulation (EU) 2021/1134 — Visa Information System reform (biometric data and border control)

question: How do the various EU border databases talk to each other?
answer: The reform mandated automated cross-checks of visa applicants against the Schengen Information System (SIS), the Entry/Exit System (EES), and the European Criminal Records Information System for third-country nationals (ECRIS-TCN). The visa application cannot proceed until all checks return a clear result. This interoperability framework is governed by Regulation (EU) 2019/817 on "interoperability between EU information systems," which was adopted in parallel to ensure technical compatibility.
title: Regulation (EU) 2021/1134 — Visa Information System reform (biometric data and border control)

question: Why did the EU expand biometric collection specifically now?
answer: The 2015 Paris attacks were the immediate trigger. Investigators found that several of the attackers had previously applied for Schengen visas using false identities, and those applications were not checked against SIS alerts because police databases were not linked to the visa processing system. The combination of the migration crisis (which overwhelmed some national border systems) and the terrorist attacks created a window for reforms that had been resisted for years due to cost and privacy concerns.
title: Regulation (EU) 2021/1134 — Visa Information System reform (biometric data and border control)

question: Why did the EU need a single set of rules for evaluating new medical treatments instead of letting countries do it voluntarily?
answer: The EUnetHTA voluntary network from 2004 to 2019 produced joint assessments, but uptake was low — only 35–40% of member states used the reports in their national decisions. The Commission’s 2017 evaluation found that duplication persisted because no member state was obliged to accept the output, allowing countries with strong HTA systems (e.g., Germany’s IQWiG) to ignore the European report and run their own processes. A regulation was necessary to create legal obligations that the previous intergovernmental structure could not enforce.
title: Regulation (EU) 2021/2282 on Health Technology Assessment

question: Why did the new EU rules for evaluating medical treatments leave out cost-effectiveness analysis?
answer: The compromise that enabled passage preserved national sovereignty over pricing and reimbursement. Countries like Germany (G-BA), France (NICE-equivalent HAS), and Denmark argued that cost-effectiveness thresholds depend on local GDP, willingness-to-pay, and budget impact — all nationally determined. Including economic evaluation would have required harmonisation of healthcare funding models, which no member state was willing to cede. The result is that joint clinical assessment remains mandatory, while economic evaluation stays under national control, preserving the core tension the regulation was designed to partially resolve.
title: Regulation (EU) 2021/2282 on Health Technology Assessment

question: Why did it take 20 years for the EU to agree on a common way to assess new health technologies?
answer: The main obstacle was political fears among member states of losing control over healthcare budgets and treatment decisions. Early proposals (2004–2010) floated a full EU HTA agency with binding authority, which was rejected as a sovereignty violation by 14 member states, particularly those with strong existing HTA systems (Germany, France) and those worried about being overruled (Poland, Hungary). The breakthrough came only when the Commission adopted a “clinical-only” scope and deferred economic evaluation. The final design also gave member states a majority on the Coordination Group, assuaging sovereignty fears.
title: Regulation (EU) 2021/2282 on Health Technology Assessment

question: What happens if an EU country disagrees with the joint evaluation of a new treatment's clinical benefits?
answer: The regulation (Article 23) does not compel a member state to accept the JCA conclusions — it only requires that the *report* be used as the clinical evidence base. A member state may still decide that a drug is not cost-effective, or that local clinical practice differs, and refuse reimbursement. However, if a member state rejects or diverges from the JCA, it must provide a formal justification published in a public database. This transparency requirement is designed to expose unjustified national protectionism or duplication.
title: Regulation (EU) 2021/2282 on Health Technology Assessment

question: Why did Germany agree to joint debt issuance for the first time?
answer: Chancellor Merkel's reversal was driven by the collapse of German export markets — 30% of German GDP depends on exports to the EU single market. With Italy, Spain, and France in deep recession, German carmakers and machinery companies faced collapsing demand. The alternative — national-level bailouts — risked splitting the eurozone and triggering a financial crisis. The historic taboos around eurobonds were broken by the pandemic's severity and the temporary, conditional nature of the fund: it was a one-off instrument, not a permanent fiscal union, and was tied to reform commitments.
title: Regulation (EU) 2021/241 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 12 February 2021 establishing the Recovery and Resilience Facility

question: Why does the EU's pandemic recovery fund tie money to reforms instead of just spending it?
answer: Years of structural funds had shown that simply reimbursing expenditures did not guarantee reform implementation. The Greek bailout programmes (2010-2018) had demonstrated the power of cash-for-reform conditionality — but those were imposed in crisis, not voluntarily. The RRF's novelty is that countries *themselves* propose reforms in their NRRPs, creating ownership. The Commission's capacity to withhold payment if milestones are missed creates a binding commitment device. This model was directly inspired by the experience of the 2013-2019 "Reform Delivery" contracts experimented with under the European Semester.
title: Regulation (EU) 2021/241 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 12 February 2021 establishing the Recovery and Resilience Facility

question: Why were Hungary and Poland's recovery funds frozen and how did they react?
answer: The July 2020 European Council compromise included a rule-of-law conditionality mechanism (Regulation 2020/2092) that allows the Commission to suspend payments if breaches affect the EU budget's financial interests. Poland and Hungary initially challenged this mechanism before the European Court of Justice, which upheld it in February 2022. Poland's funds were unblocked only after the new Tusk government adopted judicial independence measures in 2024. Hungary's case remains deadlocked: Prime Minister Orbán's government refused to implement anti-corruption reforms, and in response, Orbán vetoed EU sanctions packages on Russia and blocked Ukraine's EU accession negotiations as leverage to unlock the funds. The standoff illustrates the RRF's evolving role as a political enforcement tool.
title: Regulation (EU) 2021/241 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 12 February 2021 establishing the Recovery and Resilience Facility

question: Will the EU's pandemic recovery fund be renewed after 2026?
answer: Formally, the RRF expires in August 2026 (loans must be drawn by that date, grants by December 2026). However, the Commission's 2024-2029 strategic agenda increasingly references the need for a permanent "European Competitiveness Fund" modelled on the RRF's performance-based grants. The high-level report by former Italian Prime Minister Enrico Letta ("Much More Than a Market", April 2024) and the Mario Draghi competitiveness report (September 2024) both called for permanent RRF-like instruments for industrial policy and the green transition. A renewal faces fierce opposition from frugal member states, who argue one-off debt mutualisation was an emergency measure, not a precedent. Any extension would require a new unanimity decision from the Council.
title: Regulation (EU) 2021/241 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 12 February 2021 establishing the Recovery and Resilience Facility

question: Why did the EU need a separate health funding programme instead of just using other existing EU money pots?
answer: The Commission's impact assessment showed that previous health funding was fragmented across 14 different instruments (including the European Social Fund, the Regional Development Fund, the Health Programme) with no overall coordination. A standalone programme allows for strategic planning and cross-border crisis response that no other EU fund is designed for — the European Social Fund can't stockpile vaccines, and the Regional Development Fund can't train health workers from different member states to work together. However, the initial proposal for €9.4 billion was rejected by the Council because the Commission's own projections (SWD(2020) 275) showed that member states collectively spend €2.7 trillion annually on health — meaning even the final €5.3 billion represents just 0.2% of total EU health spending.
title: Regulation (EU) 2021/522 establishing a Programme for the Union's action in the field of health (EU4Health Programme)

question: Why did the budget for EU health funding get slashed from €9.4 billion to €5.3 billion?
answer: The reduction was the result of the classic EU "frugal vs. spender" budget battle. The Commission's May 2020 proposal of €9.4 billion was deliberately ambitious as a negotiating tactic, but the frugal four (Netherlands, Sweden, Denmark, Austria) and Germany insisted that health remains a national competence, arguing that a budget that large would create a "parallel health system" undermining Article 168 TFEU. The final €5.3 billion was agreed during the marathon 17-21 July 2020 European Council summit as part of a broader trade-off: the frugal countries accepted higher health spending in exchange for larger rebates (Germany's rebate increased from €3.1 billion to €3.6 billion annually) and the spender countries (France, Italy, Spain) got a bigger overall recovery fund. Importantly, the Commission was forced to commit that EU4Health would not create a EU health insurance system or interfere with national health delivery.
title: Regulation (EU) 2021/522 establishing a Programme for the Union's action in the field of health (EU4Health Programme)

question: Why did the European Parliament approve the EU health programme even though they said it didn't have enough money?
answer: The Parliament used the leverage it had: it delayed approval of the MFF regulation in November 2020, demanding a tripartite agreement on future budget increases. The final compromise guaranteed a mid-term review clause (Article 12) allowing the budget to be increased, and a political commitment from the Commission that next MFF (2028-2034) would have a health budget at least as large. The Parliament also secured the 20% health promotion ring-fence, the mental health priority, and a requirement that 30% of funding go to cross-border projects involving at least three member states. Rapporteur Bușoi told the plenary on 24 March 2021: "This is not the health union we wanted, but it is the one we need. We will fight for more in the next MFF."
title: Regulation (EU) 2021/522 establishing a Programme for the Union's action in the field of health (EU4Health Programme)

question: Why did the EU's main research funding programme create "Missions" instead of sticking with the old project structure?
answer: The missions concept was championed by Commissioner Moedas and modelled on the Apollo programme and the UN Sustainable Development Goals. The idea was to shift from funding research topics to achieving clear, measurable societal outcomes. The Commission's 2018 impact assessment argued that Horizon 2020 had spread funding too thinly across "bottleneck" challenges; missions would focus resources on a few visible, high-stakes targets that could capture public and political attention. The Parliament and Council broadly supported the idea, but the Court of Auditors later criticised their implementation as underfunded and poorly coordinated.
title: Regulation (EU) 2021/695 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 28 April 2021 establishing Horizon Europe – the Framework Programme for Research and Innovation

question: Why was the budget for EU research funding cut from €100 billion to €95.5 billion?
answer: The €4.5 billion cut came during the July 2020 European Council, where net contributor states (Austria, Denmark, the Netherlands, Sweden) demanded overall MFF reductions. The Commission proposed €100 billion, the Parliament supported it, but the Council initially settled on €83.1 billion. The final figure of €95.5 billion — plus the €5.4 billion from NextGenerationEU — was a compromise brokered by European Council President Charles Michel, allowing the Parliament to claim victory (total €100.9 billion with recovery funds) while the Council saved face by capping core EU budget spending.
title: Regulation (EU) 2021/695 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 28 April 2021 establishing Horizon Europe – the Framework Programme for Research and Innovation

question: Why did it take the UK so long to rejoin the EU's big research funding programme?
answer: Post-Brexit, the UK became a third country but continued to participate in Horizon 2020 until the end of 2020. The EU-UK Trade and Cooperation Agreement (signed December 2020) included a protocol on Horizon Europe association, but the EU delayed formalisation due to disputes over the Northern Ireland Protocol and the UK's refusal to pay the €2.6 billion contribution the EU demanded for 2021-2027. The deadlock was broken in September 2023 when the EU and UK reached a new financial agreement after the Windsor Framework resolved the post-Brexit trading arrangements.
title: Regulation (EU) 2021/695 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 28 April 2021 establishing Horizon Europe – the Framework Programme for Research and Innovation

question: Why does the EU fund research into new military technology instead of just buying existing weapons?
answer: The EU Treaty (Article 41 TEU) explicitly bans the EU budget from financing "the purchase of equipment" with military purposes. The EDF can only fund research, development, and prototyping—the actual purchase of tanks, jets, and ships remains a national responsibility. This legal constraint explains why the Fund covers only up to 20-30% of prototype costs: member states must pay the rest, ensuring they own the final product. The Commission's 2018 legal assessment confirmed that a full procurement fund would require treaty change, which no member state supported.
title: Regulation (EU) 2021/697 of the European Parliament and of the Council establishing the European Defence Fund

question: Why did the UK strongly oppose a joint EU fund for defence research and development?
answer: The UK, as Europe's largest defence spender (around £39 billion in 2020), feared the EDF would create a "Fortress Europe" that locked out British defence firms after Brexit. British companies like BAE Systems and Babcock relied heavily on EU-funded research and had previously dominated EU defence innovation networks. The UK also argued the Fund would duplicate NATO's Defence Innovation Accelerator (DIANA), which the UK had helped establish. The UK's exit in 2020 removed its blocking power, though the EDF regulation (Article 15) still allows third-country entities to participate under strict conditions—which the UK has not yet sought to use.
title: Regulation (EU) 2021/697 of the European Parliament and of the Council establishing the European Defence Fund

question: Does a shared EU defence fund actually make European military cooperation more efficient?
answer: Evidence is mixed. The Commission's 2023 mid-term evaluation found that 92% of funded projects would not have happened without the EDF, because member states would not have cooperated bilaterally. However, the European Parliamentary Research Service (EPRS) noted in 2024 that the Fund has not yet reduced the number of different weapon systems in Europe—the same number of main battle tank families (three) remains, though two are now co-developed among several member states. The real test—whether EDF-funded systems are actually procured and deployed—will not come until the 2030s, when the first prototypes mature.
title: Regulation (EU) 2021/697 of the European Parliament and of the Council establishing the European Defence Fund

question: How does the EU fund environmental projects on the ground?
answer: The LIFE Programme is the EU's main financial tool for environment and climate action. It provides grants (typically 60–75% co-funding) to public bodies, NGOs, and businesses for projects that implement EU environmental law, restore nature, reduce pollution, or test clean energy solutions. Funding is allocated through annual competitive calls managed by the European Climate, Infrastructure and Environment Executive Agency (CINEA).
title: Regulation (EU) 2021/783 establishing the Programme for the Environment and Climate Action (LIFE) and repealing Regulation (EU) No 1293/2013

question: Can my organisation apply for EU environmental funding?
answer: Yes, if you are based in an EU Member State or an associated country (e.g., Iceland, Norway, Albania). LIFE funds a wide range of organisations — from national park authorities to start-ups and universities. The programme distinguishes between "Standard Action Projects" (e.g., a wetland restoration), "Strategic Nature Projects" (large-scale implementation of national plans), and "Technical Assistance" (preparatory support for first-time applicants). The Commission and CINEA publish annual calls with specific eligibility criteria.
title: Regulation (EU) 2021/783 establishing the Programme for the Environment and Climate Action (LIFE) and repealing Regulation (EU) No 1293/2013

question: What is the difference between LIFE and Horizon Europe for environmental work?
answer: Horizon Europe focuses on research, innovation, and technology readiness — developing new materials, sensors, or processes. LIFE focuses on demonstration, deployment, and policy implementation — turning proven technologies into real-world practice. A Horizon project might develop a new plastic-degrading enzyme; a LIFE project might build a regional sorting plant using that enzyme. Many projects combine both: LIFE provides the pilot site, Horizon provides the technology.
title: Regulation (EU) 2021/783 establishing the Programme for the Environment and Climate Action (LIFE) and repealing Regulation (EU) No 1293/2013

question: Has the LIFE Programme made a measurable difference to the environment?
answer: Yes. According to the Commission's 2023 evaluation, LIFE projects have directly restored over 200,000 hectares of habitats, supported 1,600 Natura 2000 sites with concrete management actions, and reduced greenhouse gas emissions by an estimated 15 million tonnes of CO2 equivalent over the 2014–2020 period. The programme's most celebrated success is the recovery of the Iberian lynx from "critically endangered" to "vulnerable" — a turnaround funded almost entirely through LIFE projects in Spain and Portugal.
title: Regulation (EU) 2021/783 establishing the Programme for the Environment and Climate Action (LIFE) and repealing Regulation (EU) No 1293/2013

question: Does the EU legally require countries to fill up gas storage tanks before winter?
answer: Yes — since July 2022, EU law requires member states with underground storage to fill them to 80% of capacity by November 1 each year (90% from 2023 onward). Countries without their own storage must sign bilateral agreements with neighbours or pay for storage capacity abroad. The rule was an emergency response to Russia's deliberate cuts in gas supplies after its 2022 invasion of Ukraine.
title: Regulation (EU) 2022/1032 on measures to safeguard the security of gas supply

question: Who foots the bill for filling gas storage — taxpayers or energy companies?
answer: The regulation places the obligation on gas suppliers and traders who sell gas to consumers. But in practice, when prices soared in 2022, many companies — especially those like Germany's Uniper that had long-term Russian contracts — could not afford to buy replacement gas on the spot market. Several governments stepped in with loans, guarantees, and ultimately nationalisations: Germany spent €15 billion on gas purchases and took over Uniper, SEFE (formerly Gazprom Germania), and several storage operators.
title: Regulation (EU) 2022/1032 on measures to safeguard the security of gas supply

question: Can the EU force Russian gas companies like Gazprom to follow its storage rules?
answer: The regulation applies to any company operating storage on EU territory, including Gazprom's subsidiaries. But enforcement proved nearly impossible in practice. Gazprom Germania simply stopped injecting gas into its Rehden facility in April 2022, and German authorities had to expropriate and re-operationalise the site under the Energy Security Act. The regulation's real enforcement mechanism was that member states had to take direct control of any storage that was being deliberately left empty.
title: Regulation (EU) 2022/1032 on measures to safeguard the security of gas supply

question: Why were the storage targets set at 80% or 90% — where did those specific numbers come from?
answer: The Commission's emergency modelling showed that EU gas demand of around 360 billion cubic metres per year requires at least 30 billion cubic metres in storage to survive a winter with less than 50% of normal Russian flows. EU total storage capacity is approximately 107 billion cubic metres, so 80% fill provides about 86 billion cubic metres — enough for roughly 90 days of winter demand at normal consumption rates. The 90% target was added for subsequent years to cover a complete Russian cut-off scenario.
title: Regulation (EU) 2022/1032 on measures to safeguard the security of gas supply

question: Why didn't the EU just use existing competition rules to control big tech companies?
answer: Because Articles 101 and 102 TFEU require case-by-case proof of dominance and abuse, with investigations typically lasting 5–10 years. By the time a remedy is imposed, network effects have entrenched the dominant position. The DMA flips this: rules apply automatically to designated gatekeepers, without needing to prove harm in each instance.
title: Regulation (EU) 2022/1925 on Contestable and Fair Markets in the Digital Sector (Digital Markets Act)

question: Why do the new EU rules target only specific companies based on user numbers and revenue?
answer: The €7.5 billion turnover / €75 billion market cap thresholds plus 45 million monthly EU users were calibrated to capture exactly the handful of firms with structural gatekeeping power — primarily large US tech firms plus ByteDance and Booking — without ensnaring smaller European platforms. Gatekeeper status is also linked to controlling an "important gateway" between businesses and consumers.
title: Regulation (EU) 2022/1925 on Contestable and Fair Markets in the Digital Sector (Digital Markets Act)

question: Why should messaging apps be forced to work with each other?
answer: Because messaging exhibits extreme network effects: WhatsApp's value comes from everyone being on it. Forcing iMessage and WhatsApp to open up to competitors lowers switching costs and allows new entrants to compete, addressing the "lock-in" that traditional antitrust struggles to undo retroactively.
title: Regulation (EU) 2022/1925 on Contestable and Fair Markets in the Digital Sector (Digital Markets Act)

question: Why did the EU pass these big tech rules much faster than other laws?
answer: A rare political alignment: Parliament, Council, and Commission all wanted to be seen acting against Big Tech; the COVID-19 pandemic had highlighted dependence on digital platforms; and frustration with US enforcement gridlock created urgency. The proposal moved from publication to adoption in under 22 months, exceptionally fast for major EU legislation.
title: Regulation (EU) 2022/1925 on Contestable and Fair Markets in the Digital Sector (Digital Markets Act)

question: Why did the EU need new internet rules instead of just updating the old ones from the 2000s?
answer: The E-Commerce Directive was designed in 2000 for a simpler internet with limited user-generated content and few global platforms. It lacked obligations around algorithmic transparency, advertising, risk management, and accountability for systemic harms. Updating it would have been piecemeal and risked fragmentation across member states. The DSA provides a comprehensive, forward-looking framework that mandates proactive steps, not just reactive takedowns.
title: Regulation (EU) 2022/2065 on a Single Market For Digital Services (Digital Services Act)

question: Why do the biggest online platforms like Facebook and TikTok face stricter rules than smaller ones?
answer:
VLOPs, defined as those with over 45 million monthly active users in the EU, have outsized influence on public discourse, consumer safety, and economic activity. Their algorithms can amplify harmful content globally. The DSA's proportionality principle means stricter rules for the most powerful actors, including mandatory risk assessments, independent audits, and crisis response mechanisms. Smaller platforms face lighter obligations to avoid disproportionate burden.

**How does the DSA affect content moderation and free speech?**
The DSA requires platforms to act against illegal content, but also includes safeguards: users must be informed about content removals, have a right to appeal, and can complain to regulators. Platforms are not required to monitor all user content proactively (no general obligation to monitor), but must respond when notified. The requirement to make reasoning transparent aims to prevent arbitrary or opaque removals. Critics argue platforms may over-remove legal content to avoid fines, but the DSA includes countervailing rights for users to restore content.
title: Regulation (EU) 2022/2065 on a Single Market For Digital Services (Digital Services Act)

question: Why are some types of targeted ads being banned online in Europe?
answer: The ban on targeting minors with ads based on their personal data or on using sensitive data (race, religion, sexual orientation) aims to protect vulnerable groups from manipulation and exploitation. High-profile scandals (e.g., political micro-targeting in elections, harmful ads to teens) demonstrated how such practices can erode trust and harm individuals. The DSA also mandates that users see why they are shown an ad (transparency) and prohibits dark patterns that trick users into consent.
title: Regulation (EU) 2022/2065 on a Single Market For Digital Services (Digital Services Act)

question: How did COVID-19 change Europe's ability to handle health emergencies?
answer: Before March 2020, the EU had no power to buy masks, ventilators, or vaccines for member states. Each country negotiated alone, leading to price spikes (PPE costs rose 20x in weeks) and national export bans that broke single market rules. After COVID-19, the EU created a permanent Health Emergency Preparedness and Response Authority (HERA) that can jointly purchase medical countermeasures, maintain strategic stockpiles, and coordinate threat monitoring across all 27 member states.
title: Regulation (EU) 2022/2371 on a reinforced health emergency preparedness and response authority (HERA)

question: Can the EU force a pharmaceutical company to produce vaccines during a pandemic?
answer: No — not directly. The HERA Regulation does not give the Commission power to mandate production or redirect manufacturing lines during an emergency, despite early proposals to do so. Pharmaceutical industry lobbying (by EFPIA, Vaccines Europe) and resistance from 12 member states (led by Germany and the Netherlands) removed these powers in trilogue negotiations. The EU can use the Single Market Emergency Instrument (SMEI) to require information from companies, but cannot compel production.
title: Regulation (EU) 2022/2371 on a reinforced health emergency preparedness and response authority (HERA)

question: Who pays for Europe's health crisis stockpiles?
answer: Member states pay through national budgets for their own strategic stockpiles. The EU funds a central "resCEU" reserve (part of the EU Civil Protection Mechanism) and HERA's threat monitoring and procurement coordination — budgeted at €2.7 billion for 2021–2027. When HERA procures vaccines or treatments jointly, it typically uses a mix: EU funds for the contract (advance purchase agreements) and member states pay per dose (the "pull" mechanism). The EU also contributed €1.7 billion to COVAX for global equity, but the main financing for national stockpiles remains at member state level.
title: Regulation (EU) 2022/2371 on a reinforced health emergency preparedness and response authority (HERA)

question: Does the new EU health crisis authority replace national health agencies like France's ANSM or Germany's RKI?
answer: No — HERA coordinates but does not replace. National health agencies remain responsible for domestic disease surveillance, clinical guidelines, and procurement. HERA's role is supranational: threat scanning across the continent, joint procurement negotiation to improve buying power, and maintaining EU-level strategic reserves. The Regulation explicitly respects Article 168 of the TFEU, which gives member states primary responsibility for health policy. The compromise was deliberately crafted so no country would feel its health sovereignty was transferred to Brussels.
title: Regulation (EU) 2022/2371 on a reinforced health emergency preparedness and response authority (HERA)

question: Why do EU rules on financial cyber security regulate cloud providers directly instead of just the banks that hire them?
answer: Because the systemic risk sits with the providers, not the banks. If AWS eu-west-1 goes down, dozens of EU banks fail simultaneously regardless of how well each one managed its own contract. Brussels concluded that contract-based supervision via the regulated entity was insufficient when a single vendor underpins the whole sector — hence the direct oversight powers, modelled loosely on how central banks oversee critical financial market infrastructures.
title: Regulation (EU) 2022/2554 on digital operational resilience for the financial sector (DORA)

question: Why are these EU cyber security rules a single law that applies everywhere, rather than letting each country adapt them?
answer: The Commission and ECB explicitly wanted to eliminate national variation. NIS and NIS2 are Directives and produced 27 different transpositions; banks operating cross-border found this unworkable. A Regulation applies directly, leaving member states no room to gold-plate or under-implement. The trade-off is reduced flexibility, which Italian and Spanish smaller banks complained about during consultation.
title: Regulation (EU) 2022/2554 on digital operational resilience for the financial sector (DORA)

question: Why are crypto companies included in these EU financial cyber security rules?
answer: MiCA, adopted in parallel, brought crypto-asset service providers into the EU regulatory perimeter for the first time. It would have been incoherent to subject them to prudential rules but exempt them from operational resilience requirements — particularly given the FTX collapse in November 2022 (during DORA's final trilogue), which exposed catastrophic IT and governance failures at major crypto exchanges.
title: Regulation (EU) 2022/2554 on digital operational resilience for the financial sector (DORA)

question: Why do these EU cyber security rules require banks and firms to report cyber attacks so quickly?
answer: The ESAs argued that the existing PSD2 reporting regime — which gave payment institutions up to 24 hours — was too slow to allow supervisors to spot sector-wide patterns. DORA's tiered model (initial notification within hours, intermediate report within 72 hours, final report within one month) was modelled on the ECB's SSM cyber-incident framework piloted with significant banks in 2017–2019.
title: Regulation (EU) 2022/2554 on digital operational resilience for the financial sector (DORA)

question: Can I still be charged extra for using my phone abroad in the EU?
answer: No, as long as you are within the EU/EEA. Regulation (EU) 2022/612 prohibits any retail surcharge for roaming on a regular mobile plan. This means your domestic minute, text, and data allowances apply exactly as at home. However, there is a “fair use policy”: if you roam for long periods (typically over 4 months) or consume far more than your domestic allowance, your operator may charge a small usage fee (currently capped at €2 per GB of data, falling to €1 by 2027).
title: Regulation (EU) 2022/612 on roaming on public mobile communications networks within the Union (Roaming Regulation)

question: What happens if I travel outside the EU? Are those countries covered?
answer: Outside the EU/EEA, EU roaming rules do not apply, and roaming charges can still be high. However, some non-EU countries (e.g., Switzerland, Andorra, San Marino) have bilateral agreements that effectively extend RLAH. In other countries, your domestic operator sets its own rates. The regulation does encourage operators to offer affordable “roaming pass” options for non-EU travel, but they are not required to.
title: Regulation (EU) 2022/612 on roaming on public mobile communications networks within the Union (Roaming Regulation)

question: Why do some cheap mobile plans not include free roaming?
answer: The regulation applies to all “public mobile communications networks”, but operators can still design their domestic plans in a way that minimises roaming risk. For very cheap, data-only SIMs (e.g., €5/month for 1 GB), the fair use policy may be applied from day one, effectively excluding them from free roaming. The Commission’s 2022 impact assessment noted that about 5% of plans on the market do not include free roaming due to such design. If an operator uses a “la carte” pricing (e.g., pay-as-you-go without a fixed allowance), they must still offer a base roaming allowance equal to the domestic value of the same amount of money.
title: Regulation (EU) 2022/612 on roaming on public mobile communications networks within the Union (Roaming Regulation)

question: Can my operator ever bring back roaming charges?
answer: Yes, but only under strict conditions. The “sustainability mechanism” allows a national regulator to approve temporary roaming surcharges if an operator can prove that its wholesale costs for providing roaming exceed its roaming revenue by a significant margin (at least 3% of total roaming revenue). Only one operator (Digi in Romania) has successfully used this mechanism, and only for a short period during the pandemic. The 2022 regulation made the threshold higher and requires annual reporting. In practice, given the political backlash and regulatory burden, reintroducing surcharges is extremely difficult and unlikely.
title: Regulation (EU) 2022/612 on roaming on public mobile communications networks within the Union (Roaming Regulation)

question: Does EU law still fund new natural gas pipelines across borders?
answer: No, not since June 2022. The TEN-E Regulation explicitly excludes natural gas and oil infrastructure from eligibility for Projects of Common Interest (PCIs), which means no new gas pipelines can get EU fast-track permitting or EU funding. Existing gas projects already on the PCI list before June 2022 could be completed, but no new ones can apply.
title: Regulation (EU) 2022/869 on guidelines for trans-European energy infrastructure

question: Which countries opposed stopping EU support for gas infrastructure?
answer: Poland, Hungary, and Czechia voted against the gas exclusion in the Council, arguing they needed gas infrastructure as a transition fuel away from coal. They were joined by MEPs from Bulgaria, Romania, and Slovakia in the European Parliament. The gas industry, including Eurogas and the IOGP, lobbied heavily against the change, warning it would harm energy security.
title: Regulation (EU) 2022/869 on guidelines for trans-European energy infrastructure

question: What kind of energy projects can now get EU priority status?
answer: Hydrogen pipelines, electrolysers, and storage; offshore wind grid connections in each EU sea basin; smart electricity grids that enable cross-border electricity flow; and carbon dioxide transport and storage networks. Each project must also pass a "sustainability test" showing it contributes significantly to the EU's 2050 climate neutrality goal.
title: Regulation (EU) 2022/869 on guidelines for trans-European energy infrastructure

question: How much EU funding is available for cross-border energy projects?
answer: Under the 2021-2027 budget, the Connecting Europe Facility for energy has €5.8 billion. Projects of Common Interest are the main beneficiaries. Individual grants vary, but hydrogen and offshore grid projects have received between €20 million and €800 million per project in the first two application rounds under the new regulation.
title: Regulation (EU) 2022/869 on guidelines for trans-European energy infrastructure

question: Can the EU cut trade preferences for a country that violates human rights?
answer: Yes. The 2023 GSP regulation allows the European Commission to investigate serious and systematic violations of human or labour rights in any beneficiary country. If confirmed, the Commission can partially or fully withdraw tariff preferences. The procedure now takes about six to nine months from launch to final decision, faster than under the previous rules. The EU first exercised this power in 2020 against Cambodia, and the new regulation codifies and accelerates that process.
title: Regulation (EU) 2023/1010 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 19 May 2023 applying a generalised scheme of preferential treatment for developing countries (recast)

question: What must a country do to get better trade terms under GSP+?
answer: Countries with a lower per capita income can apply for the special incentive arrangement (GSP+). They must ratify and effectively implement 27 international conventions covering human rights, labour rights, environmental protection, and good governance. The 2023 recast added climate-related conventions, such as the Paris Agreement and biodiversity treaties. Applicants also need to accept a monitoring mission by the Commission and submit regular reports. Once granted, GSP+ provides duty-free access for about 66% of all tariff lines.
title: Regulation (EU) 2023/1010 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 19 May 2023 applying a generalised scheme of preferential treatment for developing countries (recast)

question: Does the new regulation affect trade with China or other large exporters?
answer: No. The GSP only applies to low- and lower-middle-income countries as classified by the World Bank. China was a GSP beneficiary until 2015 but has since graduated because its GDP per capita exceeded the threshold. The 2023 recast did not change graduation rules. However, the regulation indirectly affects trade dynamics because it strengthens conditionality for developing countries that compete with Chinese exports in sectors like textiles and electronics.
title: Regulation (EU) 2023/1010 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 19 May 2023 applying a generalised scheme of preferential treatment for developing countries (recast)

question: Why did the EU make climate a condition for trade preferences?
answer: The 2023 recast aligned the GSP with the European Green Deal and the EU’s goal of climate neutrality by 2050. The previous regulation required only ratification of environmental conventions, but the new version demands “effective implementation” and includes climate-specific benchmarks. This change was pushed by the European Parliament’s Greens/EFA group, who argued that trade preferences should not reward countries free-riding on climate action. The Council accepted it after the Commission demonstrated that virtually all GSP+ applicants already ratified the Paris Agreement, making the requirement low-cost diplomatically.
title: Regulation (EU) 2023/1010 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 19 May 2023 applying a generalised scheme of preferential treatment for developing countries (recast)

question: Why do new EU safety rules for machines specifically mention AI and software updates?
answer: Because the 2006 Directive defined "safety functions" as hardware-based (emergency stops, guards, two-hand controls). As software increasingly controls safety-critical functions — automatic braking in forklifts, collision avoidance in drones — the old framework had no legal lever to require validation of code changes. The March 2018 VW accident demonstrated that a routine software patch could bypass safety logic without triggering any regulatory obligation.
title: Regulation (EU) 2023/1230 of the European Parliament and of the Council on Machinery

question: Why did the EU switch from letting each country write its own machine safety laws to having one rule for everyone?
answer: Member states had transposed the 2006 Machinery Directive into national law with substantial variation — Germany required third-party certification for certain machinery, the UK allowed self-declaration, France demanded French-language technical files. This fragmentation cost cross-border sellers an estimated €152 million annually (Commission 2018 study). A regulation eliminates transposition differences and creates a single set of rules directly available in 24 official languages.
title: Regulation (EU) 2023/1230 of the European Parliament and of the Council on Machinery

question: Who lobbied hardest against the cybersecurity requirements for machines sold in Europe?
answer: The agricultural machinery sector (CEMA, the European Agricultural Machinery Association) and the construction equipment sector (CECE) argued that tractors and excavators with minimal digital connectivity should be exempt. They ran a 2021 campaign warning of "€12 billion in hidden costs across Europe" — a figure the Commission's Regulatory Scrutiny Board rejected as "not supported by any cost modelling." The exemption was ultimately denied; instead, the regulation includes a "low-risk machinery" category with lighter cybersecurity obligations.
title: Regulation (EU) 2023/1230 of the European Parliament and of the Council on Machinery

question: Why replace the 2006 battery rules with a whole new law instead of just updating them?
answer: The old directive was designed for a world of AA batteries and laptop packs. It said nothing about EVs, grid storage, or supply chains. A patchwork of amendments would have been unworkable, and a regulation (directly applicable) was preferred over a directive to avoid 27 different national interpretations of complex technical rules.
title: Regulation (EU) 2023/1542 concerning batteries and waste batteries

question: Why do new EU battery rules force manufacturers to make batteries user-replaceable, even though people like waterproof glued-in designs?
answer: Because the Commission concluded that planned obsolescence via battery degradation was the single largest driver of premature smartphone replacement. The environmental cost outweighed the design preferences. Manufacturers can still meet IP-rated waterproofing — they just need to engineer it differently.
title: Regulation (EU) 2023/1542 concerning batteries and waste batteries

question: Why do battery rules now include carbon footprint requirements?
answer: Because batteries are extremely energy-intensive to produce, and Europe wanted to avoid a situation where its EV transition was powered by coal-fired Chinese gigafactories. By making carbon footprint a market-access criterion, the EU rewards cleaner production — which happens to favour European and Korean producers running on cleaner grids.
title: Regulation (EU) 2023/1542 concerning batteries and waste batteries

question: Why do new battery rules require a digital passport for each battery?
answer: To enable a circular economy. Without standardised data on what's actually in a battery, recyclers cannot efficiently process it, second-life applications are hard to develop, and consumers cannot make informed choices. The passport also makes due diligence enforceable: regulators can trace a battery back to its mineral sources.
title: Regulation (EU) 2023/1542 concerning batteries and waste batteries

question: How can EU police get data from US tech companies when investigating a crime?
answer: Under the new e-Evidence Regulation, a prosecutor or judge in any EU member state can issue a European Production Order directly to a US-based company (like Google or Meta) if the company offers services in the EU. The company must respond within 10 days (or 8 hours in emergencies) by providing the data or challenging the order in court. This bypasses the traditional mutual legal assistance process that required going through US authorities and took months.
title: Regulation (EU) 2023/1543 on European Production Orders and Preservation Orders for Electronic Evidence in Criminal Matters

question: Can police demand anyone's personal data without a good reason?
answer: No. The regulation requires a judicial authorization—a judge or independent authority—for each order. Orders for content data (like emails) can only be issued for serious crimes carrying at least three years' imprisonment. A judge must determine that the order is necessary, proportionate, and respects fundamental rights. Providers can also challenge the order in the issuing state's courts.
title: Regulation (EU) 2023/1543 on European Production Orders and Preservation Orders for Electronic Evidence in Criminal Matters

question: Why did it take eight years after the Paris attacks to make it easier for police to get digital evidence?
answer: The delay stemmed from a three-way conflict: law enforcement demanded fast access, privacy advocates warned of mass surveillance risks, and US tech companies feared being caught between EU and US law. The 2019 ECJ ruling *Privacy International* provided legal certainty but also stalled progress. The pandemic's spike in cybercrime and political pressure from the European Council broke the deadlock in 2022.
title: Regulation (EU) 2023/1543 on European Production Orders and Preservation Orders for Electronic Evidence in Criminal Matters

question: What happens if a tech company refuses to hand over data when ordered by European authorities?
answer: The regulation imposes fines of up to 2% of the company's annual worldwide turnover for non-compliance, calculated by the issuing state. However, the company can challenge the order in the issuing state's courts, forcing a review. The conflict with US law remains unresolved: companies may face sanctions in the US for complying or fines in the EU for refusing. The EU-US Data Access Agreement aims to resolve this, but its practical application is still being tested.
title: Regulation (EU) 2023/1543 on European Production Orders and Preservation Orders for Electronic Evidence in Criminal Matters

question: Why did the EU let countries give more subsidies to chip factories instead of just paying for them with EU money?
answer: The EU budget simply does not have the firepower — the entire seven-year Multiannual Financial Framework is around €1.2 trillion, while a single leading-edge fab costs €10–20 billion. Member states, particularly Germany, hold the purse strings for industrial subsidies, so Brussels' realistic option was to relax Article 107 TFEU state aid restrictions and let national capitals write the cheques. The "first-of-a-kind" designation gave legal cover for what would otherwise have been illegal subsidies to a single company.
title: Regulation (EU) 2023/1781 establishing a framework of measures for strengthening Europe's semiconductor ecosystem (Chips Act)

question: Why is the EU's goal to make 20% of the world's chips seen as unrealistic?
answer: Global chip demand is growing roughly 8% annually, so even doubling absolute European output leaves market share roughly flat. To actually hit 20%, Europe would need to roughly quadruple capacity by 2030 — requiring perhaps €500 billion in cumulative investment, far beyond what has been mobilised. The April 2025 European Court of Auditors report flagged this arithmetic gap explicitly and criticised the Commission for not modelling it before announcing the figure.
title: Regulation (EU) 2023/1781 establishing a framework of measures for strengthening Europe's semiconductor ecosystem (Chips Act)

question: Why did Intel cancel its German chip factory even with EU subsidies?
answer: Intel's foundry business burned through cash as it struggled to compete with TSMC on advanced nodes, and the company posted catastrophic results in mid-2024. Even with €9.9 billion in German subsidies, Magdeburg required Intel to commit roughly €20 billion of its own capital at a moment when activist investors were demanding spending cuts. The pause exposed a structural weakness of the Chips Act: subsidies cannot compensate for a company's underlying competitive position.
title: Regulation (EU) 2023/1781 establishing a framework of measures for strengthening Europe's semiconductor ecosystem (Chips Act)

question: Why did it take so long for EU law to protect things like Murano glass and Limoges porcelain?
answer: The 1992 GI regulation intentionally excluded non-agricultural products because EU agriculture policy dominated intellectual property debates at the time. For 30 years, wine and cheese producers had the strongest lobbying power—their products were worth €54 billion annually by 2020, according to the Commission—while craft industries were fragmented and lacked a powerful Brussels voice. Only after the 2021 Carrara marble court ruling exposed the legal gap, and after COVID-19 boosted demand for "authentic local" products, did the political will finally unite.
title: Regulation (EU) 2023/2411 on the protection of geographical indications for craft and industrial products

question: Can a company from outside Europe register an EU protected name for its handmade products?
answer: No. Only products that originate within the EU (or approved trade partners) can qualify for EU GI status. However, non-EU countries can apply for their own GIs to be recognised in the EU under the new system—a provision designed to facilitate trade agreements. The first non-EU filings are expected from Switzerland (for Solothurn watchcases) and Turkey (for Iznik tiles), after bilateral negotiations.
title: Regulation (EU) 2023/2411 on the protection of geographical indications for craft and industrial products

question: What happens if a product uses a protected regional name but only some of its parts come from that area?
answer: The regulation allows "processing steps" to occur outside the GI region if the final product's essential characteristics come from the named location. For example, a Carrara marble lamp could be assembled in Germany if the raw marble is quarried and carved in Italy. This flexibility was the key concession that won over German and Dutch consumer goods manufacturers who feared supply chain disruption. But full manufacturing in the GI zone remains the default requirement for most products.
title: Regulation (EU) 2023/2411 on the protection of geographical indications for craft and industrial products

question: Does this new EU protection for regional crafts create extra costs for small workshops?
